title: Review: The Kona Process 134 CR DL is a Comfort Zone Companion
author: Jessie-May Morgan
article_text: Kona Process 134 Review https://cdn.jwplayer.com/previews/AVvcV9G4 Copied Loading ad Live 00:25 00:00 08:43 Cut from the same cloth as its longer-travel sibling, the Process 134 is Kona's answer to the needs of the modern day trail rider. Now in its 3rd generation, it rolls up with a new-found versatility with capacity to run a 29" or MX wheelset, with a subtly modernized set of geometry figures that don't oversell its intentions. Lo and behold the bike possesses 134mm of rear wheel delivered via the same linkage-driven single-pivot platform seen on the Process 153, and the Process X enduro bike. The range-topping 134 CR DL reviewed here is a carbon-framed affair priced at $4,499, with one more affordable carbon option, and two budget-oriented aluminum options, too. They share the same wheel size versatility as the carbon frame. Process 134 G3 Details • Carbon frame • 134mm travel / 140mm fork • 29" & MX capable • Head angle: 65.5º • 76.6 - 76.9º seat tube angle • Reach: 430 - 515 mm • Chainstay: 435 mm • Weight (actual): 14 kg / 30.8 lbs (Size S, stock) • S - XL • Price: $4,499 USD • Warranty: 3 years • konaworld.com Contents Introduction Frame Details Geometry Suspension Design Specifications Setup Climbing Descending Technical Report What's the Best Value? How Does it Compare? Pros & Cons Pinkbike's Take It's not hard work to enjoy riding the Process 134, but it will reward an aggressive and precise riding style. Its firm ride feel and quick handling through turns can make you feel pinned on smooth bike park terrain, but it's easy to come undone when carrying that speed into looser, steeper terrain where a little more suppleness on- and off the brakes would make things a little more comfortable and predictable. — Jessie-May Morgan Four holes in the front allow for either-or cable routing Frame Details The 134 CRDL has a carbon frame, and weighs in at 14 kg (30.8 lbs) in a size small. That's a little portly for a carbon bike in this travel category, but that has to be considered alongside the component specification and asking price before a fair judgement can be passed. The bike has a respectable build spec (though it is a little outdated), and its most expensive variant is clear of a thousand dollars cheaper than the low-end build options from competitors like Pivot. Personally, I am a huge fan of the frame shape and the contouring of its carbon tubes. It almost looks as though it has been sculpted by an artist at the seat tube and along the stays. Those lovely shapes can't possibly be born purely born out of function, can they? Love letter penned, let's turn to what matters. A trough, for when you run out water on a rainy ride The dropper cable makes a small appearance before entering the seat tube All sizes can accept a water bottle in the front triangle. On the small, I had to make use of an offset mount from Apidura to shift the bosses up the downtube such that the shock piggyback would clear the bottle. Accommodation made, I can fit a 600 ml bottle in there with a side-entry bottle cage - if I wrestle it in. Such a palaver might not be experienced by anyone riding the entry-level alloy Process 134 as it runs an in-line shock. Cable routing is internal and guided throughout by carbon tubes for easy cable replacements. The CR/DL model I tested has a SRAM Transmission AXS drivetrain, but there are ports for routing a cable should you ever want to go back to a mechanical drivetrain. Close to where the cable would exit the front triangle, there is a clamp for securing it on the main pivot so the cable shouldn't move and rub the carbon as the suspension articulates. Only the routing of the brake hose is external along the chainstay. Tube-in-tube cable routing, a dropped stay and comprehensive chain slap protection make for a very quiet bike. Is there anything more joyful? Some final important frame details: an ISCG 05 mount is there for the mounting of a chain guide and bash guard, and the maximum chainring size is 36T. The rear brake mount is sized for a 180mm rotor direct mount, though most models get a 20mm post mount adaptor for the 200mm rotor. Last but not least, the Process 134 runs a press fit bottom bracket. Geometry The Process 134 goes 5mm longer than the predecessor, matching the burlier 153 with its reach numbers. The four sizes span a generous range with reaches of 430mm, 455mm, 480mm and 515mm, paired with short seat tubes. Kona do not afford each front triangle its own appropriately sized chainstay length; all four sizes share the same rear triangle with a 435mm chainstay. Though insertion depth of the seat tube isn't quite to the center of the earth, it is respectable. The 175mm Reverb spec'd on the S is wholly sufficient for that frame's intended rider height. Drop length increases to 200mm on the M-XL sizes, and many a rider will want to swap it out for something longer. To that end, these maximum seat post insertion depths should be kept in mind: S ; 270mm, M; 270mm, L; 300mm, XL; 320mm. The head angle sits at 65.5°, ball-park for a bike of this travel bracket. The seat tube angles are steeper than those of the predecessor, but still a little off-the-back in regard to where the rider is positioned, and relative to some of its competitors. In a size large, it's an effective 76.7°. Given the all-round trail riding intentions, I can see why Kona are a little conservative on this front. I certainly wouldn't expect it to have the extra-steep, wrist-loading seat tube angles we see on longer-travel bikes, but a touch steeper wouldn't hurt, in my opinion. Head tube length increases in 11mm increments as we look up through the size range, so taller riders may not have to make too many accommodations to bring the bars up to a comfortable ride height. The flip-chip at the rocker-stay interface is a geometry correction tool to be used when switching out the rear wheel size. It corrects for the otherwise reduced bottom bracket height, and in doing so increases the rear axle's mechanical advantage over the shock, increasing the average leverage ratio. As such, you'll need to run a little more air in the rear shock when switching to the 27.5" rear wheel if you want to run the same sag. Suspension Design The Process 134 delivers its rear wheel travel via a linkage-driven single pivot, with a 185mm x 50mm trunnion mount shock. Its leverage curve is consistently progressive. In its stock configuration with a complete 29" wheelset, travel is its namesake 134mm, and the leverage curve starts at around 3.0, finishing up at just over 2.4. The leverage ratio is slightly increased with the MX wheelset, starting out just north of 3.1, and travel increasing to 140mm. All models ship with an air shock, though Kona state the kinematic works well with coil, too. Anti-squat is relatively high, sitting just north of 100% around sag. That indicates the bike's suspension should feel agnostic of pedaling inputs at this portion in the travel when winching up inclines in the easier gears. Specifications Price $4499 Travel 134mm Rear Shock RockShox Super Deluxe Ultimate RCT Fork RockShox Pike Ultimate RC2 Charger 3, 140mm Headset FSA Orbit 1.5 E ZS Crankarms SRAM GX Eagle Transmission Rear Derailleur SRAM GX Eagle Transmission Shifter Pods SRAM AXS Pod Handlebar Kona XC/BC 35 Stem Kona XC/BC 35 Grips Kona Key Grip Brakes SRAM G2 RSC, 200mm Centerline Rotors Wheelset WTB KOM Team i30 TCS Hubs DT SWISS 350 Tires (F) 29" x 2.5" Maxxis Minion DHF EXO / (R) Maxxis Dissector EXO 27.5" x 2.4" (S) Seat WTB Volt Seatpost RockShox Reverb (170mm, S) Compare to other All Mountain/Enduro/XC Test Bike Setup I'm 163 cm, and I tested the Process 134 CRDL in a size small with a reach of 430mm paired with a 50mm stem. Sizes M-XL are shipped with a complete 29" wheelset, but the small arrives as a mullet. It can still accommodate a 29" rear wheel, however I didn't try it myself. I cut the 20mm rise bar to my preferred 740mm, and rode the bike with the shock sitting at 25% sag (128 psi). I set the fork to the pressure recommendation from RockShox. I have spent a lot of time on the Charger 3 damper. As a rider who sits on the lighter end of the spectrum, I am familiar with the need to run its compression and rebound damping dials open, the latter to get the fork to recover its travel sufficiently from repeated hits. To those interested, the Charger 3.1 is a marked improvement on that front, allowing for much higher rebound speeds at low spring pressures and better traction as a result. Jessie-May Morgan Location: Aboyne, Scotland Height: 5'4" / 163cm Inseam: 30" / 77cm Weight: 127 lbs / 57.5 kg Industry affiliations / sponsors: None Instagram: @jessiemaymorgan Download the Trailforks App Testing Info I rode the Process 134 on a variety of terrain; bike park and more natural, granite rock singletrack, mostly in dry conditions. It was the preferred option over my go-to enduro bike for rides on sculpted bike park-esque trails, and rides that involved very long stretches of climbing on punishingly steep fire road to access some of the more iconic mountain-top descents on this eastern edge of the Cairngorms. I also took a liking to grabbing it for quick lunchtime laps of the local hill that is riddled with several easily-lapped hand-cut descents of mellow gradient. I rode it both within, and outwith its intended comfort zone to find where it excels, and where it leaves you wanting. Climbing The Process 134 isn't as spritely a climber as its travel numbers and fast-rolling Maxxis Dissector tire might have you think. The suspension is relatively neutral while pedaling on smooth fire roads. The bike doesn't feel particularly inefficient in that regard and I didn't feel the need for a lock-out. It's not so supple on bumpy singletrack climbs though, and I found it a challenge to prevent the rear tire from breaking traction over rocks, roots, as well as softer ground. The seated pedaling position is a little too far off the back for my liking. I have come to appreciate the upright position afforded by steeper seat tube angles, and the Kona is a little slack in that regard. I chose to push the saddle to its extreme forward position to alleviate some of the discomfort I felt in seated pedaling, but it didn't solve the problem entirely. On very steep sections of singletrack, the slack angle had me inching my way up the nose of the saddle in a bid to keep weight down through the front tire. Those riding flatter or more undulating terrain are less likely to experience an issue here. Descending The Process 134 is reasonably adept in descending most terrain. It got on well with most challenges I sent its way, though with varying degrees of finesse. With just 134mm of rear wheel travel, it would be naïve of anyone to expect the bike to slay on the most demanding technical singletrack there is, but also wrong to assume it isn't at least capable of managing without drama. I would generally steer clear of judging a bike too hard on its travel numbers alone, as it can often be rather misleading. I was very comfortable riding the Process 134 on technical, flowing singletrack from the get-go. Stood up on the pedals, the cockpit is a little on the compact end of the spectrum for me, but I quite like that in a trail bike. It maximizes the bike's capacity to feel playful, and can be helpful in preventing its rider from getting too over-confident in steeper sections of trail. The 65.5° head tube angle is a long way off from feeling twitchy, but when pointing the bike down the fall line, one can't help but feel a little vulnerable. I did benefit from raising the bar by around 20mm (relative to the position shown). The bike felt better to climb with the bar in the lower position in which it arrived, but for descending I preferred the more carefree riding style that was afforded by the higher bar. The suspension feels relatively firm off the top. I wouldn't go so far as to call it harsh, but on the whole the bike does have a certain firmness to it. For context, I have ridden more supple-feeling 120mm travel bikes. To begin with, I ran the rear suspension at 25% sag with high and low-speed compression wound to their open positions. On some bigger compressions and small hucks I was blowing through the travel and coming up against a hard bottom-out. I remedied that with an extra volume spacer, and a few clicks of HSC. This made the rear considerably more resistant to bottom-out, and considerably more supportive through the mid-stroke. With these settings, the bike came alive. It was a joy to ride on fast, flowing singletrack, and felt utterly wonderful to push and pump through berms and rollers. The rear end tracks very well in most situations, but under braking the suspension noticeably stiffens up. It caught me off guard on the first few rides, but I soon adapted, taking care to pick my braking zones effectively. It's not always possible of course, but when the opportunities arose, I felt it beneficial to brake before an array of braking bumps, and to roll through them off the brakes. It's not hard work to enjoy riding the Process 134, but it will reward an aggressive and precise riding style. Its firm ride feel and quick handling through turns can make you feel pinned on smooth bike park terrain, but it's easy to come undone when carrying that speed into looser, steeper terrain where a little more suppleness on- and off the brakes would make things a little more comfortable and predictable. The $5,499 USD Kona Process 134 CRDL - sale price $4,499 The $6,999 USD Pivot Trailcat LT Ride GX Eagle Transmission How Does It Compare? The recently-launched Pivot Trailcat LT has many similarities with the Kona Process 134, being MX- and 29" capable with a carbon frame and the same quantity of rear wheel travel. They deliver that travel through very different suspension layouts, but otherwise the two platforms share similar intentions. While the Pivot is considerably lighter (claimed weight of 13 kg in a medium, XTR), the $4,499 USD asking price for the Kona comes in well under the price of Pivot's most affordable Trailcat LT model. I won't do an in-depth analysis here, but know that you are getting a lot more for your money with the components on the Kona, as compared to the Trailcat LT. There are two sides to every coin, though; while the Pivot is sold with a lifetime warranty, Kona sell the Process 134 CRDL with a three-year warranty. The Process 134 is considerably more capable in my hands, up and down the hill, than the Pivot Shadowcat , a 27.5" wheel 140mm trail bike, despite that bike being equipped with a 160mm travel fork - versus the Kona's 140mm affair. On the flip-side, I recently rode a 120mm travel Santa Cruz Tallboy which, despite its XC bracketed travel numbers and 29" wheelset, takes the top spot in my mind as the most capable, playful bike of those mentioned here. The most relevant performance comparison comes with the Pivot Trailcat LT that I have in for review just now; it is the more capable of the two bikes, especially on steeper trickier trails and would therefore be my top choice if money were no object. Which Model is the Best Value? Of the four Kona Process 134 models, the most expensive $4,499 USD CR DL one that I tested will be the lightest build. However, dependent on exactly what you value, the one may not be the best option for you. One rung down the ladder, you can pay $3,199 USD for the 134 CR. It has the same carbon frame as the CR DL, but a subtly different build spec. It switches out the Rockshox Reverb for a TranzX RAD+ dropper (the more adjustable and reliable post, in my experience). Process 134 CR - $3,199 USD It also switches out the SRAM G2 RSC brakes for the G2 R brakes. They have the same 4-piston caliper and lever architecture, and thus the same capacity for stopping power, but they lack the bite point adjustment. Sure, it is a nice to have feature, and can help reduce intervals between bleeding. The other major differences lie in the suspension components and drivetrain. While the CRDL runs the previous generation upper-end suspension components from Rockshox, the CR model gets mid-range options from Fox. The CRDL has an electronic T-Type derailleur, while the CR model runs the SRAM GX mechanical option. The two bikes roll on the same Maxxis tires on the same WTB KOM Team i30 rims, but the CR model uses DT 370 hubs, as compared to the CRDL model's faster-engagement DT 350 hubs. If I was committed to one of the carbon models, I'd have to go for the more affordable CR model given it is a whopping $1,300 USD price difference. Process 134 DL - $2,399 Process 134 - $1,599 If carbon gives you the boke and you don't mind a weight penalty then you have two aluminum models to choose from, though both are equipped with more budget-oriented componentry. Generally, you have to look to more boutique brands if you want to find top-level componentry on an aluminum frame. Both models are going to be considerably heavier than the 14 kg CRDL model tested here, and the cable routing is completely external, but importantly they are sold with a lifetime warranty. It has to be said, $1,599 USD for a complete bike, sold with a lifetime warranty from a well-established brand is undeniably excellent value in 2025 - cheaper than some frame only options from other brands. You'll want to swap out its SRAM Level two-piston brakes almost immediately, but you'll have plenty of cash to spare for a quality upgrade. Technical Report SRAM G2 RSC Brakes: These brakes are reasonably powerful. With the 200mm Centerline rotors I was never left wanting, but my heavier colleagues think these should be thrown in the trash and replaced with the more powerful SRAM Code, or something with a similar amount of power. RockShox Reverb Dropper Seat Post: Early on the post developed undesirable sag. In its top-out position, its sags by around 20mm under my weight. Similarly, in its low position, it extends up by around 20mm, reducing clearance. This is a common issue with the mechanical Reverb, but is very quickly rectified with the Rockshox Vent Valve Tool that is supplied with the bike. Maxxis Dissector: On the few wetter rides, I felt a notable lack of bite from the Maxxis Dissector rear tire on soft climbs, with a similar notable lack of braking traction. Its tread pattern is relatively shallow and the centre tread blocks don't provide much modulation in that regard, tending to lock up with little encouragement. Kona Key Grip: The rubber compound is very firm and uncomfortable on long descents. I also disliked the raised bump at the end of the grip, as it prevents me from comfortably running my hands at the end of the bar. FSA Headset: All sizes of the Process 134 get this Orbit 1.5 E ZS headset with a 20mm top cap that raises the minimum stack height unnecessarily. Pros + Loves a flow trail + Quiet + More affordable than many of its direct competitors Cons - Seat tube likely to feel very slack for taller riders - On the heavier end of the spectrum for a carbon trail bike - Not the most proficient climber - Spec is a little outdated, likely owing to Kona's sale and inventory issues Pinkbike's Take The Kona Process 134 didn't blow me away in any department, but that's not to say I didn't have a good time riding it. Its slack seat tube angle and spec from a bygone era hold it back from greatness, and it left me wanting more predictability and comfort on rougher descents, but it was a fine companion for flattering, flowing singletrack. The value is great though, making this a solid option for those watching their pennies. — Jessie-May Morgan Visit the high-res gallery for more images from this review. Photos: Pete Scullion Posted In: Reviews and Tech Reviews Trail Bikes Kona Kona Process 134 Author Info: Jessie-May Morgan Pitfichie, Scotland Member since Oct 26, 2023 402 articles Jessie-May has been working as a mountain bike technical editor since 2018, joining Pinkbike's editorial team in 2023. In a previous life she worked in bioscience research in Edinburgh where she used microscopes to measure things about the immune system. She likes all bikes, but enduro bikes have historically been the preference. Jessie-May has made up the numbers at a handful of World Enduro races, though she is now in her 'downcountry era', and has an unhealthy obsession with handlebars.

title: Review: The Rocky Mountain Altitude C90 Has Impressive Grip & Adjustability, But Isn't the Easiest to Work On
author: Dario DiGiulio
article_text: REVIEW Rocky Mountain Altitude WORDS: Dario DiGiulio PHOTOS: Dario DiGiulio / Eric Mickelson The Rocky Mountain Altitude has been the race bike of choice for the Canadian company's enduro athletes, despite numbers that seemed fairly conservative relative to the competition. That's no longer the case, as the Altitude sees a full refresh with new geometry, suspension layout, frame features, and overall demeanor on trail. The all-mountain enduro bike is out, and the go-fast bike is in. The Altitude still sports 160mm of rear wheel travel, still has a host of adjustment features and flip chips, but is otherwise a completely new machine, save for the maple leaf on the top tube. There are alloy and carbon frame options, all with reach adjust headsets, Rocky's Ride-4 geo adjustment, and in-frame storage for the carbon bikes. Altitude C90 Details • 29" or Mixed wheels (27.5" size S) • Carbon or alloy frame • 160mm frame travel, 170mm fork • 63-63.8° head angle • 424-515mm reach • 427-450mm chainstays • 77-77.8° seat angle • Wheel size and frame geometry flip chips • Actual weight: 34.8 lb / 15.8 kg (size L) • Price: $3,999-10,999 USD • Frameset: $4,099 USD • bikes.com Contents Introduction Construction & Features Geometry & Sizing Suspension Design Specifications Setup Climbing Descending Technical Report What's the Best Value? How Does it Compare? Pros & Cons Pinkbike's Take The reinvented Altitude puts grip and comfort above support through the travel, but results in a bike that feels very well suited to steep and loose terrain at the cost of some predictability. — Dario DiGiulio Frame Details From geometry adjustments to wheel size, in-frame storage to clean cable routing, the Altitude has a ton of little frame features to touch on. Rocky pared down on the flip chip options for this bike, deleting 5 positions from their Ride-9 system to arrive at the current Ride-4. Located at the forward shock mount, it's essentially a slack/neutral/steep position adjuster with a top secret 4th position. At the lower shock mount is the rear wheel flip chip, allowing you to compensate for the geometry changes between a 27.5" and 29" wheel. The wheel size adjustment is not available on the size Small bike, which is 27.5" only. The headtube has a racetrack-shaped headset, with nice press-in cups that allow you to adjust the reach figure by 5mm forward or rearward from the neutral position. This adjustment doesn't affect any other geometry in a significant way, so it's purely to dial in the bike's fit. Easy open. Nice and roomy. The downtube contains more than just increased stiffness, it's also home to Rocky's new Penalty Box storage system. The opening is huge, the hatch is very easy to open with one hand, and the hidden AirTag integration is a clever little detail. The bike comes with two storage bags, one with some organization and one with a more cram-friendly design. Overall it's a very nicely executed take on the trend, and justifies whatever added complication was required to achieve it. Cable routing is fully guided and internal, but Rocky didn't simply punch some holes in the frame and call it good. There are bolt-on cable clamps at the front exit, allowing you to tighten the cables down in place and eliminate any rattling that might otherwise occur. While the alloy frames don't get the Penalty Box treatment, all of the other frame features are incorporated. It feels worth noting that the bikes do use a 30.9mm seatpost, which is just as standard as 31.6mm, but feels much less common on longer-travel bikes these days. Wheel size flip chip. Ride-4 geometry adjustment. Geometry & Sizing There are a few geometries to discuss here, thanks to Rocky's use of the Ride-4 geometry adjustment feature. From the Neutral position, adjusting to Slack or Steep changes the angles of the bike by about 0.3° to 0.5°, with an overall head angle adjustment range of 63 - 63.8°. The bottom bracket drop has a range of 11mm overall, with neutral sitting at 31mm in the full 29" position. Aside from the BB drop, the rear wheel size change doesn't radically affect the geometry, save for a millimeter here or there. The seat tube angle centers around 77.5°, again changing slightly depending on your Ride-4 position. Reach numbers for the four sizes are as follows: 430mm (S) / 455mm (M) / 480mm (L) / 510mm (XL). Those are the figures with the Ride-4 in the Neutral position, and with the headset adjustment in the central setting. The Ride-4 can change reach by +3mm or -5mm, and the pop-in headset cups offer +/- 5mm of adjustment. There are three rear center lengths spread across the range, none of which change with the wheel size adjustment or the Ride-4 position. Small bikes are 27.5" only, and the rear center measures 427mm. Mediums get a 440mm rear end, and Large and Extra Large bikes share a 450mm rear center length. Suspension Design First seen on the 2006 Slayer, Rocky has reintroduced their LC2R suspension system - a counter-rotating dual link design not dissimilar to the well-known VPP layout. A fairly high starting leverage of 3:1 coupled with a light compression shock tune makes for a very fluttery feeling top end of travel, while the high overall progression (36%) means things will ramp up quickly in the last 25mm of travel. 0 seconds of 5 seconds Volume 90% https://cdn.jwplayer.com/previews/kLctDGNY Copied Live 00:00 00:05 00:05 The main pivot is concentric to the bottom bracket, and each bike ships with a special tool to tighten that hardware. This requires removal of the crank and drive-side bottom bracket, but it's a straightforward and easy job. Some of the early production bikes (including mine) were lacking Loctite on this hardware, and as a result the main pivot would come loose quite easily. If you experience that issue, it's worth checking to see if any retaining compound exists on the threads. Specifications Release Date 2024 Price $9999 Travel 160mm Rear Shock Fox Float X2 Factory | Sealed Bearing Eyelet | 40x10mm F Hardware | SM = 210x55mm | MD - LG - XL = 230x60mm Fork Fox 38 Float EVOL GRIP2 Factory Series 170mm | 27.5 = 37mm Offset | 29 = 44mm Offset Headset FSA Orbit NO.85 Reach Adjust| 0mm Cups Installed | +/- 5mm Cups Included | Durable Sealed Stainless 36°x45° Bearings | 30.2mm x 41mm x 7.1mm Upper | 40mm x 51.8mm x 7.5mm Lower | 1.5" Crown Race Cassette Shimano XTR 12s 10-51T Crankarms Race Face Turbine Cinch | 32T | 30mm Spindle | SM = 165mm | MD - XL = 170mm Chainguide OneUp Chainguide/Bashguard Bottom Bracket Race Face BSA 73 30mm Pedals N/A Rear Derailleur Shimano XTR Chain Shimano XTR 12s Front Derailleur N/A Shifter Pods Shimano XTR Handlebar Race Face Next R | 780mm Width | 35mm Rise | 8° Backsweep | 5° Upsweep | 35mm Clamp Stem Race Face Turbine R | 0° Rise | 40mm Grips ODI Elite Pro Lock On Brakes Shimano XTR Trail 4 Piston | IceTec Metal Pads Wheelset Race Face ARC Carbon 31 Hubs DT Swiss 350 32h Spokes DT Swiss Competition 2.0/1.8/2.0 Rim Race Face ARC Carbon 31 | 32H / Tubeless Ready - Tape / Valves Incl Tires F: Maxxis Assegai 2.5 WT 3C MaxxGrip DD Tubeless Ready // R: Maxxis Minion DHR II 2.4 WT 3C MaxxTerra DD Tubeless Ready // Cushcore Trail Tire Inserts | Lightweight | Tire & Rim Protection Seat WTB Volt Race 142 Seatpost Fox Transfer Factory Series 30.9mm | SM = 150mm | MD = 175mm | LG - XL = 200mm Compare to other All Mountain/Enduro/XC RIDING THE Altitude Test Bike Setup Rocky's spec choices on this bike are perfect for the use case, leaving little be desired. In a way, it's perhaps the only over-specced bike I've ridden recently, with only a couple small details I'd change if it were my personal bike. Something still fairly unique to Rocky Mountain is the inclusion of Cushcore inserts from factory, meaning the already excellent DoubleDown tires that come on the bike will sport the added protection of some beefy inserts as well. The Fox Factory suspension on my test bike was sporting the Grip 2 damper in the fork, but the current production bikes will be shipping with the newer Grip X2 damper. A seemingly small change, but that new damper is something special, and makes for a stronger value proposition on the complete builds. Dario DiGiulio Location: Bellingham, WA, USA Height: 6'3" / 191cm Inseam: 34" / 86cm Weight: 185 lbs / 81.6 kg Industry affiliations / sponsors: None Instagram: @danger_dario In terms of flip chips and geometry adjustments, I ran the bike in Position 4 for the most part, which puts the head tube and seat tube angles in their steepest positions. At 63.8°, "steep" is a bit of a misnomer, but that's where I found the handling characteristics most balanced. Position 4 also puts the frame in its lowest starting leverage ratio, which suited the kinematics in a way that I'll dive into later. The reach adjust headset allows for some personal touch to the hard points of the frame, and I found the +5mm to be bang-on for my needs. The Altitude came with 29" wheels front and rear, and that's where I kept things for the duration of the test. Rocky Mountain's owner's manual has very thorough setup instructions, should you have any questions about how to get the most out of the bike. Download the Trailforks App Testing Info Go ride some hard trails, ideally with steep and natural with some features thrown in for good measure. This bike feels like it's fastest on tracks where managing speed is key to going fast - aka rough and steep trails with limited traction. This old Whistler enduro course feels like an appropriate use case, with plenty of fun trails thrown in to keep the racing enjoyable. Climbing I would describe the Altitude as a capable climber that is not at all in a rush to get to the top of the hill. I very rarely use lockout switches, but this bike warranted it on plenty of occasions. The suspension moves quite a bit under typical pedaling efforts, which provides tons of traction, but also leads to a wallowy feel on more consistent grades. With the lockout engaged, the saddle stays higher as you motor along, and the whole chassis calms down nicely to keep things feeling more efficient and manageable. In that sense, this bike could be a great candidate for the new crop of electronic suspension that essentially toggles that climb switch for you. The highest end SRAM build does include Flight Attendant, and some Rocky/Fox athletes have been seen using a Fox RAD prototype shock that might provide similar benefits. Lockout switches aside, when you're out of the saddle through technical climbs the soft suspension feel becomes a friend. The rear wheel fights for traction where you'd expect it to give, allowing you to push through awkward technical sections that may break up a descent and keep the momentum up. Overall, the geometry of the bike feels fairly well suited to the long climbs that will be typical of a bike meant for gnarly trails, though I'd put a steeper actual seat tube angle on my personal wishlist. The effective angle is solid, but things move quite rearward as your saddle height grows. For my pedaling height, this put the seated position a bit further back than is ideal for a big bike. Nothing a slammed saddle can't help with, but it's nice to not have to resort to that. Descending Rocky Mountain wanted to affirm the Altitude's position as their world class race bike, and by my estimation they stuck to the prompt nicely. This bike feels fast and aggressive in serious terrain, with adjustability to suit a wide range of rider preferences and fit requirements. The in-frame storage is best in class, and the smaller details are fairly well sorted for being a first pass at a completely new frame layout. The Altitude is not without its quirks, but in general the bike is modern and more than ready to tackle just about anything you can point a bike down. The geometry allows for intuitive handling at high speed and in slower technical sections, though I found the majority of the Ride-4 positions a bit slack for a race bike. If you're looking for something to point down steep trails and relax behind, then the 63° head angle might be a good setting, but for me it felt a bit lazy and hard to weight in flatter and tricker sections of trail. The steepest Ride-4 position is still plenty slack, and you get the added benefit of the most supportive kinematic of the 4 options. Speaking to that suspension feel, the Altitude's first half of travel is one of the more soft and active that I've ridden of late, making for a smooth and grippy ride over all manner of small bumps. However, when you start to push the bike hard and hit larger compressions at speed, that fluttery feel starts to fall apart a bit, moving through a lot of travel without much support to keep you from running into the heavy progression at the end of the stroke. For slow, steep moves into square compressions, the bike feels confidant and appropriate, which certainly suits Rocky's North Shore roots. In more undulating and dynamic terrain - where pumping is key and having some travel on tap prevents hang-ups in square-edge hits - I found myself wanting for firmer compression damping on the shock. After bracketing settings at home and in a few other locations, I found myself liking the stock shock with compression closed or nearly closed, on both the high and low speed circuits. I eventually had the opportunity to try an identical shock with a heavier compression tune, and found that suited my preferences better - plenty of grip and bump absorption, but a more consistent feel through the stroke. I reached out to Rocky to try to hone in on why such a light tune was chosen for the bike, and got a solid explanation from the product manager on the Altitude project, Ken Perras. While testing with Fox, we’ve found the CX001 compression tune to be the most suitable for the Altitude’s leverage profile and broad range of rider profiles that would purchase this platform. Ideally we would like to provide a more supportive compression tune or recommend a firmer compression setting profile with the chosen OEM tune, along with a faster rebound setting however that might suit more advanced or aggressive riders while leaving behind the rest of our customers. For riders that are looking for a more personalized setup, a conversation with their local tuner along with a re-tune performed alongside their regular service schedule is worth the money. Fussing about shock tunes aside, the rest of my time aboard the Altitude has been thoroughly impressive. I may prefer a more linear bike for this travel bracket, but the Rocky is undeniably good at generating grip and keeping the wheel stuck to the ground in steep and loose terrain. It would be an easy choice for people who regularly ride trails where controlling speed is a priority over generating it, and for zones where a standard lap includes committing features. Kudos to Rocky for settling on geometry figures that feel truly modern. Even in full 29" mode, the 450mm rear center on my size Large feels natural and intuitive to handle, requiring very little calibration to get used to. The bike is easy to slot into tricky, narrow sections of trail with just a little body english and brake control, and unsurprisingly feels very stable at speed thanks to the long wheelbase. I feel obliged to say that I've had some of my best Whistler park laps so far this summer aboard the Altitude, especially as things get a bit more beat up and rough. The suspension does a very good job of erasing braking bumps, the bike feels stable and ready to react at speed, and the huge downtube storage means I can bring at least two pre-Dirt Merchant hot dogs with me per lap. Rocky Mountain Altitude Yeti SB160 How does it compare? Both of these bikes are focused on going fast between the tape, but they're sure to be used just as much by folks looking for something capable and fun to tackle a wide variety of trails. The Rocky goes all in on adjustability, where the SB160's approach is more what you see is what you get. I don't compare these because of their close similarities, but because of how differently they approach the same problem. The Yeti's suspension is all about predictability and stability throughout the stroke, where the Altitude biases towards grip and suppleness. The Rocky's geometry is better suited to steep trails and slower speed tech, where the Yeti comes alive at pace and generates speed tremendously well. If your average ride is more about ticking off features and scraping down steep chutes, the Altitude is probably the better choice. For an all-around racer, where you'll have to find speed in flatter sections of trail and trust the bike to stay in shape through awkward rough patches, I'd go for the SB160. Which Model is the Best Value? Rocky certainly didn't hold back when it came to the variety of builds available for the Altitude, with 9 options spread between carbon and alloy frames. With the frame alone costing four grand, it follows that none of the complete builds are very affordable. That said, I do think Rocky does a great job of providing a lot of value for what you pay, with great parts featured on all the builds, and very few components you'd want to take off right away. For the alloy-framed bikes, the A50 build is a real workhorse, with solid suspension and SLX brakes and drivetrain. The C70 coil build is all you'd need to get after a season of racing, park riding, or general fun having. Again, not cheap, but there are no immediate upgrade needs. Technical Report Frame Hardware: The Altitude's rear suspension layout may look complicated, but it's even more convoluted to work on. Swapping shocks and servicing suspension pivots is about as frustrating a process as I've experienced lately, so hopefully you'll be able to avoid those procedures for a long while. There are non-keyed spacers on just about every pivot and plenty of flip chips to drop, so be sure to stock up on sticky grease if you're planning on pulling things apart. The main pivot came loose numerous times right off the bat, but it turns out the factory-spec Loctite wasn't added for my test bike's build. After adding some myself, things stayed tight, which I'm thankful for considering the fact that you have to pull the cranks and bottom bracket in order to tighten the main pivot with a proprietary tool. Shimano XTR Brakes: The initial set of XTR's that came on this bike had a lever leak that rendered them useless, despite my efforts to rebuild the internals and diagnose the issue. After a warranty replacement showed up from Rocky, the brakes worked quite well, and even made it through the test period without a wandering bite point. I'd chock this up to luck and a new step I've added to my Shimano bleed: once the system is well bled, pull the brake lever to the bar and strap it there for as long as you can spare, then do another funnel bleed at the lever. The pads rattled, as always, but some mastic tape below the fins solved that. Shimano XTR Cassette: I had a consistent creaking issue while pedaling in the largest 3 cogs of this cassette, no matter how clean, dirty, or well lubricated the drivetrain and freehub were. It turns out the culprit was a missing plastic washer between the back of the cassette and the microspline freehub body - an innocuous but important piece of clear plastic. Fox Transfer Dropper: The Transfer on this bike had a baffling rattle somewhere in the post itself, regardless of where you had the thing positioned in the travel. It was loud on its own, but when reverberated through the frame it became far more annoying. I swapped the 200mm Transfer for a 213mm BikeYoke Revive for the rest of the test and called it good. Worth noting, I've never seen this happen before, and it is the old version of the Transfer (with the dumbo ear clamp head), so the more easily rebuildable new version would allow you to open things up and take a look. Pros + Excellent geometry with meaningful adjustments + Impressive levels of grip over loose terrain + Race ready out of the box Cons - X2 shock tune feels too light for kinematic - Frustrating frame hardware Pinkbike's Take The reinvented Altitude puts grip and comfort above support through the travel, but results in a bike that feels very well suited to steep and loose terrain at the cost of some predictability. The significant adjustments that can be made to the bike allow for a tremendous amount of end user customization, and will suit people who like to play with a wide variety of setup options before settling in their happy place. A well-sorted race bike, to be sure. — Dario DiGiulio Visit the high-res gallery for more images from this review. Posted In: Reviews and Tech Reviews Enduro Bikes Rocky Mountain Rocky Mountain Altitude Author Info: Dario DiGiulio Bellingham, WA, United States Member since Dec 25, 2016 484 articles Dario has been obsessed with bikes from an early age, and has worked in the industry since 2015. With a Bachelor's in Architecture from Cal Poly, San Luis Obispo, he aims to bring that mix of creativity and technicality to his work in the mountain bike world.

title: Video: Scott Ransom vs Orbea Rallon - Which Lightweight Enduro Bike Is Best?
author: Seb Stott
article_text: Lightweight Enduro Shootout: Ransom vs Rallon https://cdn.jwplayer.com/previews/ErcuaZXB Copied Loading ad Live 00:25 00:00 08:51 What's the ultimate do-it-all MTB? If you want a long-travel bike capable of taking on the toughest enduro courses and bike parks but still want something that climbs well enough to pedal all day, these bikes should be on your shortlist. The 2024 Scott Ransom and Orbea Rallon are highly capable enduro bikes with 170 mm of suspension at each end, yet both are relatively lightweight and highly efficient on the climbs. But which is best? The models we have for this test are unapologetically premium, but both have less expensive stablemates and either one is versatile enough to be the only mountain bike you need. We have full reviews of the Orbea and Scott , but this article goes deep on how the two bikes compare. 2024 Orbea Rallon M-Team • 167 mm travel, 170 mm fork • 29" wheels or mullet • 63.5-64° head tube angle • Flip chip • 76.9°° seat angle (measured, high setting) • Reach: 510mm (XL) • Chainstay length: 440mm • Measured weight: 15.1 kg / 33.3 lb (XL) • MSRP: $7,999 US/ €7,499 / £7,999 • More info: orbea.com 2024 Scott Ransom 900 RC • 170 mm travel, 170 mm fork • 29" wheels or mullet • 63.8 - 65° head tube angle • Angleset • 77.4° seat angle (measured) • Reach: 508mm (XL) • Chainstay length: 440 mm • Measured weight: 15.5kg / 34.2lb (XL) • MSRP: $9,999 US / €9,999 / £8,599 • More info: scott-sports.com Features and details First, let’s take a look at the unique features of both bikes. The Scott was completely redesigned earlier this year, and the main story is that Scott hid the shock inside the downtube to match their other bikes, using a six-bar suspension system to make that work. They got rid of any lockout on the fork, but it still has the Trac Loc system on the shock that firms it up for climbing with a remote on the handlebar. The Orbea Rallon was subtly updated at the end of last year, becoming half a degree slacker and increasing rear wheel travel to 167 mm. One cool thing is that Orbea allows you to customise the paint and parts on their website - I specified a 35 mm rise handlebar, 40 mm stem and 230 mm dropper post. Weighed as they came, the Orbea is very slightly lighter, but part of that is down to the tire spec - the Orbea came with EXO+ tires front and rear while the Ransom had a DoubleDown out back. With the same wheels and tires fitted the weight is almost identical. The Scott is more expensive if you’re paying in dollars, but here in the UK, there’s not much in it. And while this is the top-tier Ransom, there is an even more expensive version of the Rallon which goes for $11,000 or £11,400. On the other hand, both bikes are available with less expensive build kits, but Orbea is probably offering better value at the lower end of the range. As for the models we have on test, neither could be described as good value and both fit into the money-no-object category. Specs Both have a full-carbon frame, Fox Factory suspension and alloy wheels. Both have moderately useful in-frame storage and the option to run a 27.5” rear wheel, although only Orbea will let you buy it with one. The Scott has an adjustable headset offering 1.2 degrees of head angle adjustment, while the Orbea has a flip chip to adjust the angle of the whole front triangle by half a degree and the BB height by 7 mm. But if you go for a 27.5" rear wheel, the shock extender for a mullet setup only lets you run it in low mode. The top-spec Ransom uses SRAM’s X0 Transmission gears and Code Ultimate brakes with 2 mm thick rotors that seem to improve consistency and bite. This second-tier Rallon makes do with Shimano's XT mechanical drivetrain which I have no problem with, but the Shimano XT brakes had a wondering bite point even after bleeding - I preferred the brakes on the Scott. On the subject of spec it’s worth noting that Orbea allow you to choose either these EXO+ trail casing tires from Maxxis or the downhill versions. I’d much rather have their Double Down enduro casing. That's what you get on the rear of the Scott, albeit with an EXO+ on the front, but I think this is a good all-rounder setup for most people. Climbing Both of these bikes are very impressive when it comes to climbing and covering ground. I don’t mean they’re better than you’d think considering the travel - I mean they’re better than a lot of trail bikes. They’re lighter than many trail bikes and both manage to keep pedal bob in check well even with the shocks fully open. The Orbea has a climb switch on the left-hand side. The Scott's shock/dropper remote is more intuitive to use than you'd think. The Scott has the Trac Loc lever under the bar. When you push it once it makes the air spring more progressive by reducing its volume. I barely notice the difference while climbing - the effect is more noticeable when you’re pushing into a berm or pumping. That can be for flow trails but I barely use it for climbing. Push it again though and it firms up the compression damping, which I do sometimes use for a long smooth climb, just because it’s so easy to use you might as well. Ad in a race situation, I could see that being a benefit for those mid-stage sprints. The Orbea has a climb switch too, but it’s harder to reach so it’s less useful. With the shocks left open, both bikes pedal equally well. The real difference is in the seat tube angle. With the saddle set to my pedalling height, I measured the Orbea’s effective seat angle at 76.4 (low) or 76.9 (high), while the Scott is 77.4 degrees. So even in the steeper setting, the Rallon puts your weight slightly further back than the Ransom. I did some back-to-back tests comparing the Orbea’s high and low settings on a loop with a steep climb and descent, and the high setting was better for me. In the low setting, I feel too off the back - I can’t get the power down as easily or control the front end on the steepest uphill sections. Fortunately, the high setting isn’t too high for descending. In fact, the BB is still about 7 mm lower than the Scott’s, and the head angle is only slightly steeper. I preferred the high setting on the Orbea - it’s noticeably better for climbing and I wouldn’t say it’s any worse for descending. But with the Scott, the seat angle is another half a degree steeper, and that’s what gives it the edge for me when it comes to climbing. Descending I got on well with the Ransom from the first ride. It's easy to use the external sag indicator to set the sag, though I did end up softening the spring pressures until I had around 33% sag. I also ran the compression damping fully open for the most part. This gives a nicely balanced suspension feel, with good suppleness and support deeper into the stroke. It feels settled on steep terrain and tracks the ground well enough, while still avoiding harsh bottom-outs on big landings. The extra sag also helps compensate for the BB which is a little on the high side, at least compared to the Orbea. The Rallon had me scratching my head a bit more. The shock has more adjustments but I couldn’t get it to feel as supple as the Ransom. I then measured the usable travel of both bikes by compressing the suspension with a ratchet strap, and the Orbea was only giving me 159 mm at the point where the ratchet strap was maxed out. There may be a bit more travel available if bottomed out really hard, but when tested in the same way the Scott was giving me 172 mm of travel. This probably explains a lot of the difference in suspension feel, because, on paper, the kinematics are much the same. I think the Fox X2's bottom-out bumper is making it hard or impossible to use all the travel and making the suspension feel more restricted when compared to the Scott, which slightly over-delivers on travel by my measurements. Geometry-wise, the bikes are remarkably similar. The main difference is the Rallon is a little bit lower (even in the high mode, which is what I used most often). But I never felt the Ransom was too high once I’d increased the sag to 33%. The ability to run the Rallon extra low is a potential advantage for riding especially steep trails with tight turns. But while both bikes are solid descenders with well-judged geometry, the Ransom’s suspension is noticeably more forgiving on bigger hits thanks to the extra travel, and it's easier to set up too. Verdict To sum up, I’m thoroughly impressed with the versatility of both bikes. They both allow you to attack the toughest climbs and roughest descents without feeling compromised in either setting. But for me, the Scott Ransom is the better-performing bike both uphill or downhill. The Ransom's hidden shock protects it from mud and grit, potentially reducing service requirements, but overall it's probably harder to live with thanks to its headset cable routing and two extra pivots, So which would I buy with my own money? Well neither - I’d probably look at an entry-level Canyon Strive or Merida One-Sixty for half the price, but then I am the sort of person who buys own-brand baked beans. Money-no-object? The Ransom is the best all-rounder enduro bike I’ve tested so far. Testing Info Download the Trailforks App Though I rode the Ransom in the rock and dust of Spain (pictured) I tested both bikes extensively in the mud and roots of Scotland, mostly using my local trails in the Tweed Valley. This allowed me to compare them directly to one another - sometimes riding both bikes on the same trails on the same day. It also made it possible to get a sense of how they stack up against other modern enduro bikes. I had both bikes on test simultaneously for two months so I often faced the dilemma of which to take out. It's a tough job, I know. I rode them on a wide range of terrain, including steep climbs that are more often pushed than ridden, rocky downhill tracks (e.g. "Classic" and Matador"), bermy trail centers and plenty of tight rooty tech. Seb Stott Location: Tweed Valley, Scotland Age: 31 Height: 6'3" / 191cm Inseam: 37" / 93cm Weight: 187 lbs / 85 kg, kitted Which of these bikes would you most like to own? Scott Ransom Orbea Rallon I already own the Rallon I already own the Ransom My current bike is perfect, thanks. I have no opinion, I just want to see the results. Responses: 2223 Faves: 0 Comments: 0 Posted In: Reviews and Tech Videos Reviews Enduro Bikes Orbea Scott Orbea Rallon Scott Ransom Author Info: Seb Stott Innerleithen, Scotland Member since Dec 29, 2014 526 articles Seb's been working as a mountain bike technical editor since 2015, and has reviewed hundreds of bikes and components in that time. Before that, he studied experimental physics at the University of St Andrews, where he got into the habit of measuring everything possible. Based in the Tweed Valley, Scotland, he loves riding trail, enduro and e-bikes, and is happiest when testing them back-to-back to see how they compare.

title: Review: Every Pair of MTB Shoes I've Worn This Year
author: Dario DiGiulio
article_text: Shoes can be an annoying investment to make, as they're one of the bits of kit that sees the most abuse on a regular basis. I've been guilty of forcing shoes well past their point of retirement, as once you've found a good pair it can be hard to move on. My collection ballooned this year, and left me with a bit of a pile to pick from on a daily basis. I ride just about every day, so these all got a fair bit of use, though the favorites ended up seeing more time on the pedals than others. Read on for a breakdown of each pair, who I think they'd be good for, and finally which ones came out on top. Some notes about me before we get into it, just for a frame of reference. All of these are a size 45 or 45.5, which is my standard street shoe size. I have a fairly wide forefoot, a narrow heel, and a high arch - that said I think arch support is a hoax to keep your feet soft. I've spent a lot of time pedaling in all of these, and each pair has seen a fair bit of hiking around in the woods. My preferred cleat position is about as far rearward as a typical channel allows, with the cleat as far as it can go outboard to keep my feet close to the cranks. Crank Brothers pedals unless otherwise noted. So Many Shoes https://cdn.jwplayer.com/previews/Or9DMd1n Copied Live 00:00 18:43 18:43 Contents Crankbrothers Mallet Trail First Degree Fuse AC Ride Concepts Hellion Clip Shimano GE9 Ride Concepts Hellion Elite Clip Fox Union Canyon Scampr Giro Chamber III Shimano GF8 GTX Vans SK8-High BMX Crankbrothers Mallet Trail Mallet Trail Details • Boa dial, hook & loop strap • TPU-reinforced toebox • Ripstop upper • Fiber-reinforced shank • Sizing: 35-49 • Price: $240 USD • Weight: 490g / shoe • crankbrothers.com The Mallet DH sold me on Crankbrothers shoes, and while the Mallet Trail is quite different, they uphold a positive legacy. The upper construction on these is lighter than you'd expect, but feels like it offers plenty of protection where it counts - the toe, heel, and side of foot are all well guarded. The gaiter is well executed, and keeps larger debris from working its way in. Crankbrothers touts their Match concept as being an ideal soul-bonding of shoe and pedal - while I do use Crankbrothers pedals, and like how these shoes interface, I wouldn't say it's drastically different than any of the other options here. You'll have to use some shims to space the cleat out, but once that's done it does lock into the pedal nicely. I've found the soles of these get fairly chewed up, but they remain useable even after plenty of time on the pins. High quality materials. Gumshoe gets a bit torn up. The stiffness is fairly neutral on these, suiting heavy-duty usage without sacrificing walkability. My only real complaint is that they feel a bit tall underfoot, as if you're further separated from the pedal you're standing on than with some of the other options here. Not a big issue, but a detail I noticed when switching back and forth. Pros + Durable, high-quality construction + Comfortable balance of stiffness and give Cons - Simple fit adjustments - Tall-feeling sole First Degree Fuse AC Fuse AC Details • Lace up, hook & loop strap • Agressive outsole • Seamless welded upper • Foam-reinforced tongue • Sizing: 36-47 • Price: 175 € • Weight: 492g / shoe • firstdegree-mtb.com I was initially quite excited about these, as the details, aesthetics, and feel all added up to a shoe that seemed pretty ideal for my use case. Sadly, one detail kept them from working for me: the cleat channel. For some reason, the cleat channel on these is very far forward in the shoe, rendering even the rearmost position a bit awkward feeling to me. I could deal with it and get used to the feel, but the rearward cleat position that all of the other shoes on test deliver was sorely missed here. The other annoyance with the channel is how deep it is relative to the sole - you'll have to run quite a few spacers to match these up with Crankbrothers shoes, and even Shimano pedals required some tall standoffs. Besides that detail, the shoes are quite nice. The stiffness is similar to the Crankbrothers, and the uppers are robust thanks to a smooth welded construction. The recessed laces feel safe from snags, and stay in place nicely once you've snugged up your fit. Speaking of fit, the forefoot on these is nice and wide, without adding too much bulk - points for comfort. Bungee for lace storage. Bummer about the cleat position. Thanks to the fairly aggressive outsole on these, their pedal contact is very strong. Perhaps too much if you're someone who likes to break free easily, in which case you'll have to space the cleat out even further. I liked the locked in feel, and found these to be some of the grippiest shoes for hiking and scrambling around, should you find yourself in those situations. Pros + Nice details, great fit + Grippy sole Cons - Cleat channel needs redesign - Many cleat spacers required Ride Concepts Hellion Clip Boa Hellion Details • Boa dial • D3O padding throughout • Cordura upper • Nylon shank • Sizing: 39.5-47, women's available • Price: $220 USD • Weight: 586g / shoe • rideconcepts.com Ride Concepts overhauled their lineup earlier this year, introducing a few models under the Hellion banner. I've been using the more gravity-focused Hellion Elites, and these: the Hellions. As the more trail-focused version of the two, these shave down on some features in pursuit of simplicity and lighter weight, but ultimately they just feel like the lesser of the two options. The sole, cleat positioning, and rubber compound are all great, offering solid connection to the pedal and great support for the foot. The main upper material is very robust, and offers great protection for the foot. Where these fall short for me is in the smaller details, such as with the Boa loops that started fraying almost immediately. It seems like brushing through undergrowth has been enough to cause these to fray quite a bit, and though they're holding strong so far, the design feels a bit flawed to me. Not quite as burly as their Elite counterpart. Same great sole and shank though. Luckily the general fit of these is quite comfortable, with a generous forefoot and tight heel cup. I've found that the single Boa dial isn't adequate for tightening the whole shoe down in one go, requiring you to either pull the lacing through or flex your foot around to distribute tension as you crank things down. Compared to the Elites, which use two Boas, these feel a bit less secure. A note: these and the Elites have the furthest back cleat channel of any of the shoes on test, so far rearward that I actually moved my cleat up a bit in the channel - a first in quite a while. Those who like a cleat at the forward tip of the shoe (why?) might not love this feature, through I really do. Pros + Comfortable fit, good pedal connection + Protective and generally durable Cons - Weak point details - Boa tension is hard to distribute Shimano GE9 GE9 Details • Boa dial, hook & loop strap • Torbal flexible midsole • Debris collar at ankle • Fiber-reinforced shank • Sizing: 38-48 • Price: $250 USD • Weight: 507g / shoe • ride.shimano.com I've put this pair through the wringer, partially because they were the only shoes I had on hand for a little while, partially because they are very comfortable. If you have wide feet, these are plenty accommodating, with a big toebox and a fairly loose fit. One the flip side, if you like a locked-in ski boot feel this probably won't be your ideal shoe. The material feels soft and pliable, with a fair bit of extra padding throughout. While that helps with protection and some comfort, it makes these feel a bit warmer and more prone to getting soggy. I also found that I had to alter my cleat position to keep the inner portion of the shoe from rubbing on the crank too much, due to how wide they are. Lotta' rubbin'. Nice hiking tread. Due to that rubbing, I ended up sawing through the buckle that retains the hook-and-loop strap in very little time, necessitating the addition of a little DIY slot in the upper material. That did the trick, and they've continued to work despite. The plastic Boa lacing loops are great, and assure that the laces tension up the shoe every time, despite grit, grime, and wear. Overall, the feel of the upper and sole of these is softer and more malleable than most of the other shoes on test, which makes them very walkable but takes away some of the racy stiffness some folks want. If you do want more stiffness, Shimano released the GE900HS , which is stiffer and a little slimmer than these shoes. Pros + Wide and comfy + Nice lacing, good tread traction Cons - Soft and baggy feel isn't for everyone - Strap durability Ride Concepts Hellion Elite Clip Boa Hellion Elite Details • Two Boa dials • D3O padding throughout • Cordura upper • Nylon shank • Sizing: 39.5-47, women's available • Price: $262 USD • Weight: 596g / shoe • rideconcepts.com As mentioned earlier, these are essentially the bigger, better version of the Ride Concept Hellion - hence the name. The fit felt a bit potatoey at first, but after a short break-in period they started cinching in to my foot shape and feeling like home. The sole is plenty stiff without being ridiculous, and the overall fit feels comfortable enough for all-day use. The heavy weight on the scale luckily translates to impressive durability in real world use, as these have held up remarkably well to my abuses. The laces are pulling smoothly, the upper fabric is intact, and the sole is still functioning well despite getting chewed up by the pedals. In my book, a bit more weight feels like a reasonable tradeoff for a shoe that lasts longer than just one season. Boa bumpers. Holding strong despite a tone of use. That weight also manifests in a very protective shoe, one that has certainly saved my toes on a handful of occasions. The D3O padding seemed a bit gimmicky initially, but after having some talus rocket into the top of my foot I became convinced of its utility. Same goes for the plastic toecap, which deflects nicely off of trailside obstacles. The Hellion Elites are warm and lack much breathability, but they're not unbearable even through the summer months. These have proven to be a pair that I reach for first, thanks to the well-rounded design and functional details. A note: as I mentioned about the Hellions earlier, these have the furthest back cleat channel of any of the shoes on test, so far rearward that I actually moved my cleat up a bit in the channel - a first in quite a while. Pros + Very protective + Good balance of comfort and utility Cons - Warm, not very breathable - Heavy Fox Union Boa Union Details • Two Boa dials • Welded and reinforced toebox • Ripstop upper • D3O insert, two arch support options • Sizing: 37-47 • Price: $275 USD • Weight: 506g / shoe • foxracing.com The Unions are one of the stiffer pairs in this lineup, with a sole that gives very little even under massive loads. They feel precise, with good pedal contact and sticky enough rubber to lock in. The rigid last doesn't hike very well, but does the job when need be. For me, the Unions have one critical flaw: the material of the upper comes way too far up the ankle, pushing into the tendon at the front of the ankle (tibialis anterior). The rubbing was at its worst on climbs, where the repetitive motion of pedaling really ground things down. After trying to break them in naturally, I resorted to a bit of user modification, opening up that corner with a pair of snips. This helped quite a bit, but didn't completely alleviate the issue. Comfort modifications. Rigid. Fit-wise, the Unions are on the narrower side of things, but they don't feel too uncomfortable for me. The rubberized toe cap keeps things from opening up with use, but my feet had enough room for long rides. The dual Boa dials keep things tight and well-adjusted, and while they lack some of the dial protection that other shoes feature, I haven't had any damage occur. Pros + Stiff and precise + Nice fit Cons - Annoying cuff detail - Not great for hike-a-bike Canyon Scampr Scampr Details • Boa dial • Knit upper • Tri-panel tension fit • Solestar insole, Vibram sole • Sizing: 36-48 • Price: $160 USD • Weight: 451g / shoe • canyon.com Canyon first introduced these shoes to the gravel / adventure cycling market, but from the jump they struck me as a potentially ideal trail bike shoe. The defining feature here is the knit upper, which feels like a durable sock with some structure to it. The single Boa dial cinches the whole thing together, and you're off to the races - or the graveling or adventuring, whatever. Thanks to that upper construction, the Scampr is breathable and light, with a somewhat disconcertingly airy feel. Great on hot days, but not so great when it comes to impact protection. That's entirely not the point here, so it feels like an unfair complaint, but don't expect these to protect your upper foot from much. There is a solid toe cap, and the sole offers plenty of support for real descending. These are my favorite shoe for short-travel bikes where I know I won't be pushing the limits, and for longer days in hot temps. Extreme sports sock. Stiff enough where it matters. The stretchy fit of these should accommodate a wide variety of foot shapes, and felt very comfortable to me. The fit system Canyon implemented here is excellent, hugging your foot tight without any faff. Even if the Boa were to break, the shoe's cuff is sturdy enough to keep them on your foot, in a pinch. My one complaint comfort-wise comes down to the Solestar insole, which has an odd bump right in the middle of your forefoot, and never felt quite right to me - I swapped it out for a random insole that fit, and I've been happy. Pros + Very comfortable and breathable + Excellent fit system Cons - Very little protection in upper - Solestar insole Giro Chamber III Chamber Details • Lace up, Velcro strap • Padded microfiber upper • Low-profile tongue • DH-focused shank • Sizing: 36-48 • Price: $200 USD • Weight: 538g / shoe • giro.com The Chamber III is on par with the Fox Unions as the stiffest two pair of shoes on test here, but the Giros take things a step further with a tighter and more aggressive fit and feel. The last of these is quite narrow, and might not work with those with wider feet as a result. If that doesn't disqualify them for you, then they provide a very locked-in feel, with plenty of fit adjustment thanks to the traditional lacing system and a Velcro strap to cinch things down. Giro reduced the heft and thickness of these quite a bit compared to the last iteration of the Chamber, and we're left with a shoe that feels nicely pared down, while still offering enough protection and durability. The protection here feels more like padding than a shell, especially when compared to shoes like the Hellion Elite - both work, but the shell method seems to withstand a bit more abuse. As you'd expect with a very stiff shoe, the hiking performance isn't amazing, but Giro did give these sticky enough rubber to keep you upright on all sorts of dumb scrambles. Padded faux leather upper. Real stiff, quite narrow. I found the narrow fit required some lace adjustment here and there over long days, opening up slightly with enough time. The lacing feels a bit fiddly compared to the Boa dials featured on most of the other shoes, but you can get a very secure and stay up on your bunny ear technique. As a package, these are a pretty ideal choice for those looking to put a ton of power into the pedals, and get little to no feedback from those efforts or impacts. Pros + Stiff, locked-in feel + Precise fit system Cons - Narrow fit - Fussy to adjust Shimano GF8 GTX GF8 Details • Boa dial • Gore-Tex liner • Tall elastic cuff • Low-temperature sticky rubber • Sizing: 38-48 • Price: $220 USD • Weight: 513g / shoe • ride.shimano.com Shimano's GF8 is the only dedicated mountain bike flat shoe I'm including here, because it's my favorite that I've used this year. They are far from perfect, but the important things are close to my ideal. They used a softer, low-temperature sticky rubber for the sole, which hooks to pedals remarkably well, no matter how foul the weather. The extra traction is welcome, and luckily hasn't some at the expense of a sole that lasts - these still look good despite plenty of use. The stiffness also feels well-suited to my preference, with a bit of give to allow for good pedal feel, without sacrificing impact protection. Yes, they're also waterproof, but that feels far less critical for me, despite living in a notoriously wet place. The upside is a dry foot through splashes and light rain, the downside is a permanently wet foot as soon as any water gets in past the cuff. Waterproof means it keeps just as much in as it lets out. I'm willing to live with that tradeoff, as the boot fit is nice and secure, albeit annoying to get on and off. Weak materials and a very tight cuff. Excellent rubber. The biggest complaint I've had about the GF8 GTX comes down to durability - the liner material has proven to be far too light-duty for the job. Because the cuff is so tight, you have to really yank on these to get them on (or get a shoehorn), and the very thin heel area is prone to tearing as a result. I've continued to use these well past that patch giving up the ghost, but I'd like to see better design on a pair of shoes that otherwise ticks all the boxes. Pros + Great pedal feel + Very sticky rubber, even in cold Cons - Poor durability - Frustrating to put on Vans SK8-Hi BMX SK8-Hi Details • Lace up • Duracap high-wear reinforcement • PopCush footbed • Vulcanized rubber • Sizing: 34.5-47 • Price: $90 USD • Weight: 519g / shoe • vans.com These are just a good old pair of Vans. There's nothing special and mountain bike specific about them, they just happen to be one of my favorite shoes for the job. If protection is important to you, look elsewhere, but if grip and pedal feel are your main goals, then the classic high top is a pretty compelling option. I picked these up to see if the BMX moniker actually improved anything in terms of durability and usefulness for the bike, and so far they've impressed. It's no mystery that Vans durability has been pretty horrible of late, but the rubber and uppers has held up on these so far. Though they lack any sort of upper foot protection, the UltraCush insoles do take the sting off any bottom-of-foot strikes. Crankarm protection. Classic. I don't love the pointed-toe fit of Vans, but after a long break in they do start feeling like home. A simple lace-up fit keeps you locked in if you want it, though these are anything but stiff. The beauty of Vans is how much pedal feel you get, and how well the sole holds onto pedals. They're really not optimized for mountain biking, but that hasn't stopped them from being a great option for the right use cases. Pros + All the pedal feel + Great grip Cons - Lacking protection - Pointed fit Final Choices For those looking for a great all-rounder shoe, I'd recommend the following: 1. Ride Concepts Hellion Elite Boa Clip 2. Crank Brothers Mallet Trail 3. Giro Chamber III For shorter travel bikes and lighter use, the Canyon Scampr is my top choice. For pure downhill use, the stiff and precise feel of the Giro Chamber III would be my pick, with a nod to the Fox Union despite some issues with the design of the upper. With a Bachelor's in Architecture from Cal Poly, San Luis Obispo, he aims to bring that mix of creativity and technicality to his work in the mountain bike world.

title: Review: Saris' New Edge 2" Hitch Rack
author: Dario DiGiulio
article_text: SarisEdge https://cdn.jwplayer.com/previews/FT3qWM6K Copied Loading ad Live 00:25 00:00 07:15 Bike racks come in all shapes and sizes, but a common variety seems to be emerging amongst the mountain bike community. The fold up tray rack is pretty ubiquitous, with versions from most brands out there, both small and large alike. Saris already has one offering in this realm - the modular MHS system - but they're adding a second option with the Edge. This rack is fairly simple, with one arm holding the front wheel and a strap securing the rear, but there are some little details thrown in to try to set it apart. Edge Details • 80lb weight limit per tray • Fits wheels 24” to 29” x 3” or 27.5” x 5” • No frame or fender contact • Built-in cable lock • RV rated, 2" hitch • Weight: 67lbs / 30.4kg • Price: $1000 USD • saris.com The Edge has a very high load rating per tray, and adds compatibility with fatbikes and smaller-wheeled bikes to the mix. The 80 pound limit is well in excess of what most folks will need, but consider it extra assurance should you hit a nasty bump in the road with your 60-pound eMTB on the rack. Fits long bikes. The telescoping arm has some tricks up its sleeve, with a spring-loaded design that keeps things tight, and a built in detent to make sure the hook doesn't interfere with fenders, forks, or brake lines. The spring function keeps the arm tight to the tire automatically, and all you have to do is give it a solid push to secure things for the drive. For the rear wheel, things are more typical, but there's one added detail to improve the standard ratchet design. In addition to the very long ratchet strap, there's a sliding block that allows you to raise the rear tire by 2.5", for better clearance should your saddle interfere with the other bike's handlebars. In Use Loading / Unloading The loading process is the same as with any other one-arm rack, but things are made a bit easier by the spring loaded hook and limited range of motion on the arm's pivot. You can't over-clock the arm, it just lands in the same spot every time, right in front of a long fender. The spring helps keep things snug, and is extra handy when unloading the bike and dropping the arm down - the hook quickly returns to the shortest position out of the way. The built-in lock is a nice touch. Not the most secure, but good in a pinch. I find ratchet straps to be a bit annoying to use, as you have to thread them through the spokes every time you load the bike, making sure to not trap them down with the rear wheel when loading things. Unloading is fairly easy, as you just depress the plastic catch and yank the strap out. I haven't had too many occasions to use the rear wheel spacer on the Edge, so mostly it has remained off to the side as more of a tire chock. If you should need it, the block slides around pretty easily with two hands, and stays securely in position once ratcheted down. On and Off Road This is a Honda Fit folks, don't get too excited. The biggest hinderance to off road usage on the Edge is the lack of clearance, as the rack does nothing to lift the bikes above the hitch receiver. For taller vehicles, this might not be an issue, but every car out there benefits from more clearance out back, even if just for steep driveways. The anti-rattle tightening feature works well here, and keeps the rack securely in place in the hitch, without any loosening through the test period. I find two-arm racks to be more secure in true off-road situations, as the extra rigidity that comes from the second set of arms helps reduce how much the bikes can wag. The Edge lets the bikes flop a fair bit, but hasn't done anything scary in my time with it. Part of this extra movement is probably due to the extra tire clearance in the front wheel basket, where there's a fair bit of clearance for the tire to rotate without any support on the sides. When the rack is stowed upright in no-bike mode, there is a noticeable rattle between the rack and the main pivot point, as the body rocks back and forth in the detent. This probably won't be apparent on RVs and off the back of massive vehicles, but it was hard to ignore when driving around town in my smaller car. Stowing the rack in the 3/4 position mostly alleviates this, but that's not the cleanest solution if you're looking to keep the rack folded tight to the back of your car. Easy one handed deploy, usually a two hand stow. What Could Be Improved? The aforementioned rattle is an annoyance, but could be less of an issue for some users depending on the vehicle type. My bigger complaints come down to the finer details of the rack, and the potential durability issues that might come up. The overall chassis is sturdy, with a massive pivot point and beefy trays, but the arms have some play in them that is sure to develop further over time, as pretty much all telescoping hook armed racks are wont to do. The ratchet hardware is already showing some rust, and though it hasn't affected things in use yet, it might do so after a few more months of road grime immersion. I've noticed that after heavy rains the rack drains water for a while afterwards, meaning quite a bit can pool up in the trays if the rack stays on your car. It's possible that won't amount to much in the long term, but only time will tell. My last frustration is with the ratchet straps, namely the damage they can do to the rear rim of the bike. As your bike shimmies back and forth in the rack, the tightened ratchet strap constantly rubs on the location where installed, leading to noticeable scratching of the rim, especially in wet weather or when the bike is dirty. Some ratchet designs employ a slide-on boot to prevent this, but the Edge doesn't include that preventative measure. Plus, those boots are easy to lose as you use the rack, making them something of a half measure. Durability The Edge rack has been living on my car for the past two months, and aside from the concerns listed above, things haven't changed a bit. That's far from a long term test in the world of bike racks though, and time will tell how well the Saris survives. Saris offers a limited lifetime warranty, mostly against any defects in manufacturing, excluding rust, paint damage, and wear and tear. Pros + One hand opening, slick arm design + High load rating, RV approved + No frame contact + Holds large tires Cons - Cost is high for middling quality - Ratchet straps are fussy and can scratch wheels - Rattles when stowed upright Pinkbike's Take The Saris Edge packs a bunch of features into a fairly sleek package, but lacks some refinement when it comes to finish quality and elegance. The design of the front arm makes loading quick and easy, and easily avoids frame contact, but the ratchet strap is a little fussier and can scratch rims. Though it wouldn't be my top choice for a regular car, folks with extremely heavy e-bikes and an RV might find it ideal. — Dario DiGiulio Visit the high-res gallery for more images.
