title: TRP EVO Pro Brake Review: The 'Set and Forget' Favorite Now Has More Adjustability
author: Dario DiGiulio
article_text: TRP Evo Review https://cdn.jwplayer.com/previews/bisBJiM6 Copied Loading ad Live 00:25 00:00 01:31 TRP EVO PRO TRP's brakes have been on a steady upwards trajectory over the past few years, with the DH-R EVO cementing the brand as one of the main competitors in the space. The recently updated EVO PRO is a refinement of the previous model, adding adjustments at the lever and modifying the ergonomics to better suit the wide array of preferences out there. They piggyback on an existing caliper design, meaning existing users can upgrade the lever without replacing the entire system. EVO PRO Details • Mineral oil • Adjustments: Reach, pad contact • 2 pad options • Price: $616 USD (Black, pair) • Brake weight: 315g (front) • Rotor weight: 224g (203mm) • trpcycling.com BUY AT: Thunder Mountain The EVO PRO is easily associated with their loud gold anodization, but are also available in silver and black finishes, for those that want a stealthier look. TRP uses mineral oil in their brakes, and states that they are only to be used with their 2.3mm thick rotors. Setup The TRP bleed procedure is quite easy, and requires very little upkeep to maintain performance. On the whole, these are some of the best brakes for those that simply want to set them up once and never think about them again, thanks to a robust design and lack of required maintenance or adjustment. TRP recommends a piston massage while initially setting up the brakes, in order to get all four pistons firing at the same time. You can use an old brake rotor or a plastic tire lever to gently move them back in after advancing them, with only a couple cycles needed to get things in sync. New to the EVO PRO is what they've dubbed the Pad Activation Dial, which allows the user to change how sharp the initial bite of the brake is. You can make the bite firm and solid, or soft and easier to modulate, all via a dial inside the lever. The levers still implement a simple reach adjust mechanism, with a far wider range of reaches than the previous DH-R EVO. Though neither Matt nor I experienced any issues with leaking on these brakes or a few other test sets, I have heard of such issues from other users. Livability: Easy funnel bleed, very long service interval. Good lever adjustment, with meaningful change to feel and performance to suit different users. Feel The previous generation of TRP's gravity brakes - the DH-R EVO - was very well received, save for one issue: the ergonomics simply didn't work for some users. The lever reach was quite far from the bar, even at the minimum adjustment, and didn't suit those with smaller hands. The EVO PRO solves for that, implementing a more aggressively hooked lever shape and a revised reach adjuster that allows for a wider usable range. It might not be their calling card, but TRP has achieved a nice light lever feel that doesn't have as much of a learning curve as brakes like the Hayes Dominion. There's little resistance when pulled, but it's still easy enough to intuit where you are in the stroke and get a feel for when the power will come on. That activation point is incredibly consistent, with no change in the throw across long runs and lengthy service intervals alike. Feel Ranking: Light lever action that doesn't feel vague or too feathery. The sturdy and stiff feel gives them a strong feel under force, and works well with their extremely consistent bite point. Water Bottle Pull Test: 503 g pull force (4th overall) As you'll see in the full-length video review, this test simulated the force required to get the brake to the bite point. Water was incrementally added to a bottle attached to the brake's lever, until bite force was achieved. Power The power on tap with the EVO PRO doesn't feel drastically different to that of the DH-R EVO, but don't take that as a negative. The TRP feel is fairly average when it comes to reefing as much power as possible out of the brakes, but that is more than enough to control speed in extreme terrain, especially if you don't have perfect on/off braking technique. The initial bite isn't as sharp as the strongest options like the Mavens, Saints, or Hopes, but once you pull into the stroke the power ramps up nicely. It's worth noting that these new brakes are just a revised lever, with the caliper remaining the same. That caliper houses four 16mm pistons, and is compatible with either lever option, should you want to swap between the two. We ran the blue Performance Resin pads for this test, matched up to the RS05E rotors. Those ultra-perforated rotors add quite a bit of power to the system, and are a notable upgrade for those running the older rotor design. The Resin pads have a nice initial bite, all the more so if you're very diligent about bedding them in correctly - we found that this played a notable role in how much power was felt at the wheel. All brakes benefit from patient bedding, but the TRPs seemed a bit more sensitive to a rushed job. Power Ranking: Mid pack in this group, but adequate for any serious riding. The RS05E rotors go a long way in increasing power, and are a solid upgrade for the older DH-R EVO as well. Honda Fit Stop Test: 27.4 m / 90 ft (5th overall) As you'll see in the full-length video review, this test was meant to highlight the raw stopping power of each brake. We attached Matt to the back of Dario's Honda Fit, revved up to a consistent speed, at which point Matt used only the bike brakes to slow the vehicle to a measured stop. Pros + Easy setup, low maintenance + Approachable power + Good ergonomics Cons - Lacks some initial bite compared to top options - Good setup critical to max power - Old model (DH-R EVO) is just as good Pinkbike may earn commission on any sales through affiliate links. Learn more . With a Bachelor's in Architecture from Cal Poly, San Luis Obispo, he aims to bring that mix of creativity and technicality to his work in the mountain bike world.

title: Review: Fox Union All Weather Flat Shoes -  For the Wettest Days
author: Matt Beer
article_text: Fox Union All Weather Shoes https://cdn.jwplayer.com/previews/8RShqB0K Copied Loading ad Live 00:25 00:00 06:56 These shoes aren’t messing about. Look at the size of that gaiter - that’s the first thing a waterproof shoe should have. Without a healthy overlap under your pant cuffs, your toes soon start to swim in foot juice, no matter how impervious the shoe itself is. The Fox Union All Weather Flats are essentially a gumboot specifically designed for riding bikes. They share a similar construction and appearance to the other Union Boa shoes in Fox’s lineup, at least below the ankle. One small difference is that the All Weathers only features one dial to lock your foot in place. Union All Weather Flat Shoes • Boa Fit System closure • extra tall gaiter • Ultratac outsole • D3O insole • Sizes: 37-47 (41-46 in half sizes) • Colors: black, adobe • Weight: 550 grams (per shoe, size 41.5) • MSRP: $329.95 USD • foxracing.com When it comes to grip on the pedal pins, the All Weathers call on the Ultratac rubber, like the standard Union Boa Flat shoes. If flat pedals aren’t your jam, there’s a clip version that looks identical, aside from the cleat bed on the sole. Puddles are irrelevant to these shoes. Ultratac rubber is decently grippy. The concave sole detracts from the otherwise tactile connection to the pedals. Overlap is key. Thankfully, Fox figured that out and went sky high. Price and Weight For how much wet weather gear costs, we sure do treat it poorly. You wouldn’t dare jump in a bog with your white Jordans, but that’s exactly what the Union All Weather shoes are ready for. That being said, $325 USD is a lot of dough. Waterproof socks are a decent alternative, but the name isn’t as accurate as one might expect - they will get wet and clammy. You’ll also lose the close connection to the pedals with those thick, rubbery socks. These hydrophobic moon boots aren't tremendously light at 550g per shoe. However, much of that is owed to the mega-high and very functional gaiter. I don’t necessarily see that number as a hindrance while riding, but it may bother the weight weenies. Pull tabs front and back - they'll come in handy. So far, the stitching hasn't missed a beat. Bunching can occur in forward foot movements. Fit Pulling these puppies on takes practice and patience; getting them off was nearly impossible at first. I nearly separated the back of the sole trying to step on the opposite foot. Sitting down and wiggling out of them in stages is the best method I’ve found. They will break in, ever so slightly. Still, it’s a comically strenuous job to remove them. Once they’re on, the gaiter has a comfortable level of snuggness once your foot is planted. I found the fit true to size, just like all of the other Union Boa shoes I’ve tried - typically, I go for a size 8.5 US in riding shoes. Considering these will be worn in the winter with thicker socks, and the width of Fox shoes in general isn’t the most roomy, I could see bumping up a half size being necessary for riders with wide feet. Fox also provides a second set of taller arch supports. I found the lower option worked best for me. With the pant cuffs rolled down or zipped up, it's hard to differentiate the Union All Weather shoes from summer kicks. Performance Like I said, wet weather gear is a hefty investment. Should the ride connection be given up for gaining waterproof qualities? First, it’s unfair to assume waterproof shoes ride like standard summer issue shoes, especially those with insulating properties. Two major elements are being added to resist the weather, so the extra material will take away from the dexterity. Those features are well taken care of. However, some level of tactile contact with the pedals is crucial for control and less binary for those who choose to clip in. So, what about that all-important “feel”? Looking lengthwise down the sole underside, you can see that it has a concave shape. I’ve come across this conundrum before with a pair of Scott SHR-ALP BOA and wondered why they were so hard to find the perfect spot on the pedals with. Once you do find the LZ, the grip is decent. The tread isn’t as tacky or deep as that found in Five Ten’s special Stealth Rubber formula, but it’s not far off. I’m a fan of the Boa Fit System on pretty much any piece of riding kit. It may not be the Boa laces' fault entirely, but the gaiter material behind the highest piece can scrunch up and cause a pinch point in extreme downward heel flexion. The upper materials of some waterproof shoes are fragile. These are tough and showing little signs of wearing out. So far, so good - no holes from pedal pins. Durability I’ve given these a thrashing already through a drastic shift into fall here in Squamish, but I’m hoping they break in further and the sole flattens out. In that area, the rubber still looks brand new, despite my tendencies to chew through the flat pedal shoe soles at a specific point from the pedal pins. Judging by the two years I’ve spent bashing on the Union BOA Clip shoes, I’m confident that Fox has nailed their TPU rubber joining methods and carried that forth on the All Weather model. Two vulnerable areas I thought might wear quickly are behind the uppermost BOA lace crossing and the cuff’s pull tab stitching. The gaiter is made from an incredibly tough material, but neither area has shown any weakness. How Do They Compare? Fizik Nanuq GTX: $299 USD. The sole is too stiff and the cuff verges on being too low, but with the right pair of pants, you're feet will be dry and warm through the deepest slush. Shimano GF800 GTX: $220 USD. If dexterity and grip are your main focus, these Shimano shoes are among the best. It's a shame they aren't warmer or legitimately waterproof. Five Ten Trailcross GTX: $200 USD. This model from Five Ten is an interesting concept; they're light, flexible, and almost waterproof. Grip and control are high, as you'd imagine from anything bearing the Five Ten logo. Endura MT500 Burner: $189.99 USD. Another strong contender in the warm and waterproof realm, however, the rubber tackiness sits lowest on the scoreboard against these other options listed. Pros + Fully waterproof materials. + Water is less likely to splash under the pant cuff thanks to the extra-tall gaiter. + Sleek design - no velcro to wear out or laces to tangle. Cons - Extremely difficult to remove. - Not as tactile on pedal surfaces as other less water-resistant options out there. - Forefoot gaiter fold can bunch up when leaning far forward. - Performance comes at a high cost. Pinkbike's Take This is the wet-weather riding shoe, or boot rather, that I've been waiting for. In the Union All Weathers, there's no longer a need to avoid puddles - just pick the best riding line and you'll stay dry. They're not perfect, but I'm willing to give up a fraction of grip and feel on the pedals for the rest of their hydrophobic properties. — Matt Beer Visit the high-res gallery for more images from this review. Posted In: Reviews and Tech Reviews Shoes Fox Clothing Fox Union Author Info: Matt Beer Squamish, BC, Canada Member since Mar 16, 2001 607 articles Matt has been a Pinkbike reader since the site's early days. He discovered mountain biking in Newfoundland, where a small community of dedicated riders and trail builders set him on a path to earn two Canadian National Downhill Championship titles. Now based in Squamish, BC, Matt works as a tech editor for Pinkbike, specializing in gravity-focused mountain bike reviews, tech, and racing coverage.

title: Review: Shimano GF800 GTX Flat Pedal Shoes - Grippy & Ready for the Rain
author: Matt Beer
article_text: The GF8GTX has all of the features of a premium wet weather flat pedal shoe: sticky soles, Boa closure, ankle gaiter, and Gore-Tex. There aren't many similarities between derailleurs and shoes, but that hasn’t stopped Shimano from producing quality products in both categories for decades. One of their staples has been the MW5, a waterproof clipless shoe. Until recently, an equivalent flat pedal version has been hard to come by. Shimano has answered with the GF800 GTX, delivering all the bells and whistles needed in a waterproof flat shoe. It features a Gore-Tex shield throughout, an ankle gaiter to keep debris out, a Boa dial closure, and a specially formulated rubber sole. GF800 GTX Details • Boa dial closure • Gore-Tex gaiter ankle cuff • Ultread-GR • Added toe protection • Sizes: 38-48 • Weight: 433 grams (per shoe, size 42) • MSRP: $220 USD • shimano.com Shimano has worked diligently to ensure that the Ultread GF rubber compound remains flexible and grippy in cold weather when those materials tend to firm up. No laces to get clogged with mud. This Ultread rubber was designed to remain soft in colder weather. The sole is more flexible compared to previous Shimano flat pedal shoes I've tried. Price and Weight The GF800 GTX is Shimano’s exclusive waterproof flat pedal shoe in the lineup, and it doesn't come at an insignificant cost - $220 USD to be exact. But who can put a price on dry feet? A single shoe weighs 433g in size 42 or M8.5. Shimano includes a one-year warranty and free shipping on orders over $100. There’s also a free return and exchange program offered for that price. You also get the option of a fully black shoe or one with a splash of olive color. When lined up against a direct competitor such as the Five Ten Trailcross Gore-Tex, the Shimanos cost $20 more and weigh slightly more. They’re certainly not heavy, though, and I’d say the price is in the ballpark for a shoe with these features. The ankle gaiter has an elastic cuff to keep debris out but this soft material isn't exactly "waterproof". Overall, the shoe is not cumbersome as you might expect for a wet weather shoe, however, the toe has a stiffer rubber cap for protection. Fit The GF8GTX only comes in full sizes from 38 to 48 - no half sizes or wide options exist in this model. Shimano does add a note stating that if you previously used the ME line of shoes, size down. Otherwise, if you were running the AM models, keep the same size. Typically, I run a size 42/8.5 and found them to be a touch narrow and short, but extremely comfortable. Another note on winter riding is that you’ll probably choose thicker socks, so keep that in mind. Putting on the GF800 GTX's is a bit like sliding into those popular leather boots you see in every mountain town. The Boa dial makes them convenient to remove with cold or muddy hands and the elastic ankle cuff, which is neither too tight nor too loose, features finger loops to slip on easily. The entire lacing system relies on just one Boa cinch system with three lace loops per side of the shoe. Wisely, Shimano has placed the Boa dial slightly higher on the shoe to prevent it from snagging on low-lying objects and also included a small guard to shield the dial. Good coverage and breathable, but not the warmest for winter riding. Extra grip at the toe and heel edges work well for digging in when hiking up or down. Performance Grip is not the most important thing when it comes to clipless shoes, but for a flat pedal enthusiast, it is first and foremost. Thankfully, Shimano has made improvements from the GR9 shoe and specifically kept cold weather riding in mind for the GF800 GTX. Not only is the rubber designed to remain soft and sticky in cooler temps, but the sole of the shoe is also flexible enough to conform around the pedal slightly without giving up support underfoot. The GF800 GTX shoes aren't quite as sticky as Five Ten's benchmark Stealth rubber, but they're not far off, and I don't have any complaints about how well my feet stayed on the pedals. In terms of construction and protection, they meet a favorable middle ground. They're light, fairly breathable, yet still have a protective toe cap where it matters most. As for the rest of the shoe, I found the ankle area to be slightly shallow. Even though the sole is thin and wide, which is preferred for flat pedals, my heel sat high against the soft walls of the shoe. The retention and area that the laces capture is excellent however, especially considering there is only one Boa dial. Staying warm and dry is a crucial part of the equation here and the GF800 GTX does okay in those categories. Having enough overlap on your pants is critical in order to stay as dry as possible - the more, the better, because the upper material is not the tallest or 100% waterproof when submersed - it's more like splash proof. On rainy days, I'd end up with mildly wet feet shortly into a ride compared to something as impervious as the Fizik Artica X2 . The GF800 GTX shoes do a decent job of keeping water out without turning your feet into a total steam room though. The trade-off is that there isn't much insulation. Even with dry feet and wool socks, I'd end up with chilly toes on cooler days soon than I anticipated. All in all, I'd reach for these shoes when there's any chance of standing water and much prefer the dexterous, grippy GF800 GTX over the bulk of a waterproof sock in a regular flat pedal shoe. I tend to wear out shoes on the inside from rubbing the crank arm while descending, but the GF800 GTX shoes have been robust in gritty conditions. High performance rubber, whether it's in a tire or a shoe, tends to wear faster. These shoes have seen a lot of use this fall, but they're holding up well - most importantly, they're grippy. Durability Sticky rubber can degrade quickly at the sacrifice of increased grip, but so far that hasn't been the case with the GF800 GTX. They've latched on to pedal pins well without being totally shredded. I’ve noticed that I can be hard on the shoe panel that rests against the crank arms, particularly in winter months when gritty mud is prevalent. All of the materials have held up well without any signs of fraying at the seams. They've also staved off plenty of sticks while bashing through the underbrush and the Boa closures never accidentally disengaged. Pros + Flexible, thin and grippy sole clings to pedals in cold weather + Lightweight yet protective toe cap + Boa lace system is secure and shielded from impacts Cons - A deeper heel cup would provide further ankle support - Upper gaiter material is only water-resistant - "Cold-weather ready" refers rubber remaining soft, not so much the insulating qualities Pinkbike's Take Shimano has answered the call of flat pedal enthusiasts when it comes to an appropriately grippy and flexible shoe. The only small complaint has to do with the height of the ankle gaiter - an even taller cuff would improve the shoe's ability to keep water out. The GF800 GTX isn't cheap, but it hits the mark when it comes to comfort, grip, and overall performance, making it one of my top picks for wet weather riding. — Matt Beer Posted In: Reviews and Tech Reviews Shoes Shimano Shimano Gr800 Gtx Author Info: Matt Beer Squamish, BC, Canada Member since Mar 16, 2001 607 articles Matt has been a Pinkbike reader since the site's early days. He discovered mountain biking in Newfoundland, where a small community of dedicated riders and trail builders set him on a path to earn two Canadian National Downhill Championship titles. Now based in Squamish, BC, Matt works as a tech editor for Pinkbike, specializing in gravity-focused mountain bike reviews, tech, and racing coverage.

title: Review: Hope Tech 4 V4 Brakes - Strong and Sensitive
author: Dario DiGiulio
article_text: Hope Tech 4 Review https://cdn.jwplayer.com/previews/TPOtuzju Copied Loading ad Live 00:25 00:00 00:55 Hope Tech 4 V4 The heavily machined, often colorful look of Hope brakes always stand out on a bike, with a cult following devoted to the British brand's high-end stoppers. The Tech 4 V4 has been their benchmark gravity brake for some time now, earning a spot on DH, freeride, and enduro bikes alike. Though they've since introduced an additional heavy hitter in the gravity lineup - namely, the EVO GR4 - the Tech 4 V4 remains one of the best brakes money can buy. Read on for our long-term impressions, as well as some comparisons to the new GR4. V4 Details • DOT fluid • Adjustments: Reach, bite point • 5 pad options • Tested: Green Race pads, 2.3mm thick 200mm rotor • 2x18mm, 2x16mm pistons • Price: $628 USD (pair) • Brake weight: 285g (front) • Rotor weight: 222g (203mm) • hopetech.com BUY AT: Competitive Cyclist Setup Hope's bleed process is a bit less refined than the other options on test, requiring some additional faff to install the bleed cup atop the master cylinder. That said, it's a repeatable and time-tested method, with solid results and only some cleanup to worry about. The Hope-branded DOT fluid is easy enough to come by, and we found that they never needed a re-bleed after initial setup. There are five pad options and a few different rotors to choose from, opening up a wide array of tuning opportunities to the end user. As with all of the brakes on test, we asked the brand to send us the most powerful combination of both - in this case, that was the green Race pads, and their 2.3mm thick 200mm rotor, without the fancy floating aluminum carrier. The Tech 4 lever features both reach and bite point adjustment, which work somewhat dependently with each other, meaning you'll likely be trimming in one setting after altering the other. No complaints here, as the desired setup position is still easy to achieve, and a fairly short deadband can be achieved at any reach adjustment. One setup annoyance plagues the Tech 4 V4, and that has to do with the width of the caliper body. Hope's own website calls this out, and it's reflected in real life, where some bikes and forks struggle to accommodate the caliper's width. If your frame has an unusually tight rear brake area, or you're hoping to run the V4 with a 180mm rotor up front, it's best to check with Hope or do a test fit beforehand. Livability: The Hopes are some of the easiest to live with on test, thanks to a fuss-free hydraulic system, easy alignment, and solid adjustments. The caliper fitment could be an issue for some, but we experience no problems. Feel Hope achieved a very light lever action with the Tech 4 V4, but preserved a nice level of feedback, allowing the user to sense where they are in the stroke without much of a learning curve. The long lever was comfortable and easy to setup for both Matt and I, but we both noticed a bit of flex within the lever when you're really reefing on them. That long lever gives you a smooth stroke, with no break-in force required to get the action moving. Once you hit the bite point, power comes on with a progressive ramp that feels aggressive enough to achieve max power quickly, but still feather things lightly when desired. The bite point is perfectly consistent, and doesn't shift with heat buildup. One complaint that feels adjacent to feel is the noise the Tech 4 V4 caliper makes when braking. Best described as a screech, this high-pitched noise isn't alleviated with caliper alignment or any bed-in procedure, and is just something to get used to. Likely to bug some and be a non-issue for others, it's ultimately just the sound of them working. Feel Ranking: The light lever action feels intuitive and very easy to control, giving the Hope's an accurate and precise feel. With a quick ramp up past the bite point, you can brake efficiently, but still feather the lighter end of that power when needed. Water Bottle Pull Test: 493 g pull force (3rd overall) As you'll see in the full-length video review, this test simulated the force required to get the brake to the bite point. Water was incrementally added to a bottle attached to the brake's lever, until bite force was achieved. Power The Tech 4 V4 balances power and sensitivity in a way that should appeal to many riders. With a fairly progressive feel, you can get up to max power quickly, but still control that initial bite point. Compared to brakes like the Maven or Saint, the Hope feels less grabby, but still offers similar power in sustained pull moments when you're just working to slow the bike down. Fans of a very aggressive ramp in power might prefer those other options, but the Tech 4 V4 is not to be ignored here. The Race pads offer a the best initial bite of their lineup, and are impressively resistant to fade. The screeching noise is an unfortunate byproduct, but worth it for maximizing the brake's performance. Power Ranking: Strangely, the Hopes didn't fare as well in our perfectly scientific Honda Fit Stop test as expected, slotting in 7th place. That said, their feel on trail is impressive, with accessible power that feels adequate for any gravity use case. Honda Fit Stop Test: 30.2 m / 99 ft (7th overall) As you'll see in the full-length video review, this test was meant to highlight the raw stopping power of each brake. We attached Matt to the back of Dario's Honda Fit, revved up to a consistent speed, at which point Matt used only the bike brakes to slow the vehicle to a measured stop. The new GR4. Updates Hope's newest gravity brake takes many cues from the Tech 4 V4 and introduces some refinements for user experience and ease of service. The EVO GR4 features 2x17mm and 2x18mm pistons, but still uses the same pad shape. The lever is compatible with three different lever blades, the longest and straightest of which (dubbed: Power) best mimics the feel of the V4. All of the lever options have a bit more of a hook to them, which some users will likely prefer. There is an increase in power, and less flex in the system, yielding a firmer feel through the lever blade. The lever action feels slightly less light and free into the stroke than the V4, but still has a consistent and easy motion to it. Both remain excellent options, but the implementation of some additional aftermarket tunes and the addition of a threaded bleed port on the lever will probably lead some users to the GR4. The new model seems to be less screechy, but the noise from pad friction hasn't totally been eliminated. Pros + Sensitive, accurate lever feel + Massive power + Easy to modulate Cons - Lever flex - Screeching pads - Wide caliper won't fit all setups Pinkbike may earn commission on any sales through affiliate links. Learn more . Posted In: Reviews and Tech Big Brake Test Reviews Brakes Hope Hope Tech 4 Author Info: Dario DiGiulio Bellingham, WA, United States Member since Dec 25, 2016 484 articles Dario has been obsessed with bikes from an early age, and has worked in the industry since 2015. With a Bachelor's in Architecture from Cal Poly, San Luis Obispo, he aims to bring that mix of creativity and technicality to his work in the mountain bike world.

title: Review: 7iDP's New Project 21 Open Face Helmet
author: Henry Quinney
article_text: 7iDP have brought to market a new open face helmet. While this isn't their first foray into this style, this is a higher end prospect than the current M1 model. The naming convention looks to bring it inline with their Project 23 full face helmet that we reviewed several years ago. The 21 includes some of the same design cues as well as technologies. The Project 21's claimed weight of 402 grams (+/- 10g) is around 50-80 grams heavier than some of the lighter weight trail helmets available. My test M/L helmet weight 406g. Although 7iDP say the name is derived from the amounts of vents, it stands to reason, and a bit of guesswork, that there might be a Project 22 3/4 helmet at some point. Project 21 Details • 7iDP Rear Dial Adjuster • ERT (Energy Reduction System) • Conehead Foam • 21 vents • Crash Replacement Policy • Weight: 406 grams (M/L, actual) • Sizes: XS/S, M/L, XL/XXL • Meets CE, CPSC, AS Standards • MSRP: $229.99 USD • www.7idp.com The helmets 7iDP's ERT (Energy Reduction Technology) system. This is the same tech that used to be called SERT. This is used to reduce rotational forces transitioning from the helmet to your head. It also uses Conehead foam, which uses two layers of foam that intersect with each other via a lot of corresponding cone shapes, with a decoupling layer in between. These cones then break or crush under the impact, which is claimed to reduce harmful forces to a rider's head. Lastly, the helmet uses a three-position visor designed to release in the case of an impact. The brand also uses size-specific visors for aesthetic reasons. For the record, I think this is a great-looking helmet, so maybe there's something in it. The helmet has 21 vents, with large ones crossing just above the rider's brow. The Trail Pro model on test comes with a zipped-carry case that more typical of a full face helmet. (I've actually been looking for one of these for a while. While flying, I normally like to take my helmet as a personal item in the cabin. The bag conveniently fits a laptop in the base, and can mean you have a bit more wiggle room in terms of weight). Project 21 uses different size pads to help fine-tune the fit. The strap uses a magnetic Fidlock buckle and a ratchet dial to hold your head securely. It is available in sizes XS/S, M/L, XL/XXL in their Black Hologram Colourway. There is also a crash policy, where if you crash in a helmet, you can buy a replacement at 50% off. Fit and Function This is a really deep feeling helmet, and reminds me of the TLD A3. It has that comfy-bucket feel. In some helmets, especially deeper ones such as the POC Kortal, I find that they come too low, and I can almost see the brim through my eyebrows. This isn't the case with the Project 21, even when the ratchet is at its second highest position, meaning the front of the helmet is angled down more. I am typically on the cusp between Large and Extra Large helmets, and have a head size of 59cm. I found the M/L to be an excellent fit, with plenty of room for adjustment and all while using the thicker pads. While not heavy, it does feel more sturdy on your head. The weight is noticeable compared to my favoured Bluegrass Rogue, with the 7iDP helmet feeling a lot deeper around the back. I would say that it's not the most ventilated helmet, though, especially compared to something like the Bluegrass. All in all, this is a great-looking helmet that is both reassuringly sturdy, very comfortable and with a lot of coverage to boot. Helmet reviews are tricky because we never want actually to test them in terms of safety, that's not to say that some don't feel more robust than others. My only gripe is that I found it hard to find secure and easy eyewear storage in the vents with glasses that have slightly bent arms. They could be made to fit, but not easily enough to do one-handed while riding. Pros + Comfortable, deep fit + Feels sturdy and secure + Comes with a carrying case Cons - Slightly heavier than other similar helmets - Middle-of-the-road ventilation - Sunglasses storage can be challenging depending on arm shape Posted In: Reviews and Tech Reviews Helmets 7idp Author Info: Henry Quinney Squamish, BC, Canada Member since Jun 3, 2014 362 articles

title: Hayes Dominion A4 Brake Review: Ultra-Light Lever Action and Plenty of Power
author: Matt Beer
article_text: Hayes Dominion Review https://cdn.jwplayer.com/previews/E2wMU6Qd Copied Loading ad Live 00:25 00:00 00:46 Hayes Dominion A4 In the late 90s and early 2000s, the Hayes Mag brake was heralded for its immense power. The purple and champagne color brakes were one of the most popular choices for downhillers. In that era, fully kitting out your bike with Hayes brakes likely included buying aftermarket CNC'd Dangerboy or Razor Rock levers, too. The hype slowly faded when Hayes built some less successful brake models, and other competition came along from Hope, Shimano, and Avid. After a hiatus from the gravity side of the sport, Hayes launched the Dominion A4 back in 2018. It would be fair to say that they were met with some skepticism. That was short-lived - positive reviews began to emerge and Hayes rebuilt a loyal fan base with the Dominion A4s. Two factors that make the Dominions stand out to gravity riders are their extremely light lever action and the manner in which they quickly build power. In terms of structure, the ambidexterous master cylinder houses a reach adjustment screw and a tool-free bite point dial built into the lever. At the caliper end, a couple one-of-a-kind setup aids can be found. Another bonus for consumers is that Dominions include a set of semi-metallic and sintered pads in the box. Brake Details • DOT 5.1 fluid • 4x17mm pistons • Pad contact adjustment • Tool-free lever reach adjustment • Crosshair caliper alignment feature • 2-stroke caliper bleed feature • Semi-metallic and sintered pads included • 160, 180, 203, 220mm rotors avail. • Bronze, silver, and purple colorways • Weight: 303g front, 189g rotor • Price: $499.98 USD (pair) • hayesbicycle.com BUY AT: Competitive Cyclist Setup Hayes chose DOT 5.1 fluid for the Dominions, a decision that’s becoming less common with brands like SRAM and Formula moving away from the caustic oil. The bleed process uses two syringes to move the fluid through the system (available separately). Hayes has shared a step-by-step procedure of how to bleed the entire Dominion brake , or just the caliper end of the system using the 2-Stroke dual bleed ports. Each half of the caliper has its own bleed port to move fluid in isolation from the rest of the system. This can be handy to purge the smallest amounts of air. Another unique feature found on the Dominions is the Crosshair alignment system. Two set screws help to keep the caliper centered over the rotor while tightening the mounting bolts. Each complete brake comes with a 180cm length hose. Although I haven’t experienced it myself, the kevlar reinforced hose is larger in diameter than most and can be stubborn to feed through some tube-in-tube carbon frames with internal routing. Hayes offers four rotor sizes ranging from 160mm to 220mm in a six-bolt mounting pattern only. At 1.95mm, they aren't nearly as thick as some of the other hefty discs out there. Liveability: I've heard of some riders breaking a Dominion master cylinder clamp in a crash, but they've survived any lay-downs I might have had using these brakes. With the exception of easily accessing the pad contact adjuster, working on the Dominions is generally headache-free. Feel The ultimate brake test is to walk over to any bike and grab hold of the lever. In all seriousness, when you do this with a set of Dominions, you’ll immediately notice how freely the lever moves inward - that showed in our lab experiment too. In fact, the lever action is so light that it can take some getting used to modulating the brake while trying to hold a wheelie. The lever is shorter in length compared to the Trickstuff or Hope brakes on test, and the pivot is further away from the bar too. A bevelled edge positions the hook of the lever comfortably under your finger. Adjusting the reach is taken care of by the tool-free dial built into the lever, while tuning the pad contact, or bite point, requires turning the screw located behind the lever with a long-ended 2 mm allen key. Feel Ranking: These brakes surprise everyone who gives them a squeeze. Shape-wise, they're similar to the TRP DH Evo lever but with the easy-on feel of the Hope Tech 4 V4s. Their low-friction pull and short engagement sets them apart from any other brake out there. Water Bottle Pull Test: 447 g pull force (1st overall) As you'll see in the full-length video review, this test simulated the force required to get the brake to the bite point. Water was incrementally added to a bottle attached to the brake's lever, until bite force was achieved. Power The Dominions reach their potential quickly, and that’s a good thing. Some riders may perceive the low lever force to be vague at first, but once the pads have made contact with the rotor, there is excellent, intuitive modulation. Reaching full power takes very little effort and is maximized when using the sintered pads. The most distinguishable difference from the semi-metallic pads is heat dissipation - the sintered pads have a far greater ability to deliver power when they’re running hot on prolonged steeps. The championing characteristic of the Dominion A4 is how effortlessly you can deliver stopping traction to the tires. That should equate to less tired arms on the trail - you’ll just have to recalibrate your brake reflexes when balancing wheelies. Power Ranking: The Dominions are up there in terms of power simply because of how mindless it is to achieve such a high braking force. They delivered in the Honda Fit Stop test too, falling in behind the two big bosses of power: the Shimano Saints and the SRAM Maven Ultimates. Honda Fit Stop Test: 25.9 m / 85 ft (3rd overall) As you'll see in the full-length video review, this test was meant to highlight the raw stopping power of each brake. We attached Matt to the back of Dario's Honda Fit, revved up to a consistent speed, at which point Matt used only the bike brakes to slow the vehicle to a measured stop. Pros + Smooth, gradual power curve. + Extremely light lever movement. + Clever setup and bleeding features. + Great value. Cons - Extremely light lever movement. - Pad contact adjuster is annoying to access. - Pads rattle. Pinkbike may earn commission on any sales through affiliate links. Learn more . Posted In: Reviews and Tech Big Brake Test Reviews Brakes Hayes Hayes Dominion Author Info: Matt Beer Squamish, BC, Canada Member since Mar 16, 2001 607 articles Matt has been a Pinkbike reader since the site's early days. He discovered mountain biking in Newfoundland, where a small community of dedicated riders and trail builders set him on a path to earn two Canadian National Downhill Championship titles. Now based in Squamish, BC, Matt works as a tech editor for Pinkbike, specializing in gravity-focused mountain bike reviews, tech, and racing coverage.

title: Review: Formula Selva V – Are 35mm Stanchions Still Enough for Enduro?
author: Matt Beer
article_text: Formula Selva V Review https://cdn.jwplayer.com/previews/OP3J1aCR Copied Loading ad Live 00:25 00:00 09:26 When Formula set about designing a new Selva V single-crown enduro fork, they never bought into the 38mm stanchion trend that Fox, RockShox, DVO, and X-Fusion all rushed toward. Formula held their position on 35mm stanchions with the theory that forgiveness in a fork chassis is desired. The Selva V’s travel can be set to 160, 170, or 180mm, making it ideal for enduro or eMTBs. $1,298 gets you all the tools and auxiliary pieces to keep you busy tinkering on the internal adjustments. Externally, there are just three dials: a singular rebound and compression adjuster, and the seldom-seen fork lockout lever. The focus of this fork, besides the narrow tubes, is the highly adjustable Compression Tuning System (CTS) damper design. This is a truly unique way of tuning the damping with plug-and-play compression circuits. With eight possible tunes, the Selva V can yield drastically different ride characteristics, but is the CTS an over-engineered solution to a problem that doesn’t exist? Selva V Details • Travel: 160, 170 (tested), 180 mm • Adjustments: rebound, compression, lock-out gate • Redesigned air spring • 35 mm stanchions • Designed for 29" wheels • Offset: 43 mm • Maximum rotor size: 220 mm • CTS damper w/interchangeable shim stacks • Integrated fender • Weight: 2,125 g (w/ axle, lever, fender, uncut steer tube) • Price: $1,298 USD / €1,250 EUR • More info: rideformula.com Is bigger always better? Formula doesn't think so - the Selva V only has 35mm stanchions. Air Spring and Damper Fox’s new GlideCore air spring moves radially on elastic mounts to reduce friction for a more sensitive ride. Formula does the same on the Selva V, except they use it on both the air spring and the damper. It’s something they call Internal Floating Technology. Externally, the Selva V doesn’t appear to have a ton of adjustment; there’s just one rebound and compression dial, but don’t let that fool you. Major changes are accomplished by changing the CTS valve or pre-assembled shim stack. There are eight options in total, which are divided into two series of compression force curves: the Traction (progressive) and Support (digressive). It’s enough to keep a full-time suspension tuner busy, but changing the CTS valves is not as daunting as one might think. A proprietary tool unthreads the colour-coded CTS valves without the need to rebleed the damper. It’s not something you’d likely do on the trail, but they can be swapped faster than an air volume spacer. On that note, the air spring holds another intriguing component, part of any Formula air fork: the NeoPos air volume reducer. Unlike plastic spacers used in some other forks to adjust air spring progression, the foam-like NeoPos reducers compress as the fork moves through the travel to produce a shallower end-stroke force curve. A svelte crown for the Selva V. Compression in blue, lockout "gate" in black. A lockout lever isn't that useful on an enduro bike - you want the fork to compress and steepen the seat tube angle while climbing. Rebound is on the bottom of the lower leg in red. A short fender is included; bleed buttons are not. Chassis 35 millimeter stanchions may seem small by today's standards, but it wasn't that long ago that it was the norm on forks like the RockShox Lyrik B-series. Formula says they kept the stanchions narrower to achieve the balance of steering precision and compliance that they believe to be ideal for enduro bikes - they also make a dual-crown version, dubbed the Belva. Despite its slim looks, the Selva can handle abusive bike park riding. A new lower casting sets the bushings further apart to increase stiffness, a hot topic in the inverted world . There's no pinch bolt on the non-threaded side to grab onto the 15mm axle, though. There are eight CTS valves split into two series: Support and Traction. The gold and red are included with the fork. Individually, they cost $67 USD, or you can buy the whole series for $280, including the spare tool. Damper curves: The Support Series uses digressive compression tunes, whereas the Traction Series give progressive variations. Air spring curves: notice the sharp curve that a solid volume spacer (green) inflicts versus the shallower NeoPos spacer (orange). Escape will cancel and close the window.
