Caption Settings Dialog Beginning of dialog window. Escape will cancel and close the window. Text Color White Black Red Green Blue Yellow Magenta Cyan Transparency Opaque Semi-Transparent Background Color Black White Red Green Blue Yellow Magenta Cyan Transparency Opaque Semi-Transparent Transparent Window Color Black White Red Green Blue Yellow Magenta Cyan Transparency Transparent Semi-Transparent Opaque Font Size 50% 75% 100% 125% 150% 175% 200% 300% 400% Text Edge Style None Raised Depressed Uniform Dropshadow Font Family Proportional Sans-Serif Monospace Sans-Serif Proportional Serif Monospace Serif Casual Script Small Caps Defaults Done Internal Floating Technology by rideformula Add to Favorites Added to Favorites Views: 2,789 Faves: 0 Comments: 0 Spring on the left. Damper on the right. Formula includes everything you need to dive into the tuning setup, from the proprietary top cap tool, Neopos volume reducers, two CTS valves, and fork oil. Changing either the CTS valves or the number of NeoPos volume reducers can transform the ride characteristics of the fork. Price and Weight There is no base model Selva V, which means it comes at a premium price of €1,250 EUR, the same cost as a RockShox Zeb Ultimate. At that price point, I’d prefer a fork with a traditional setup process, one with a wider degree of external adjustment that I can change on the trail. All of Formula’s products do include a two-year owner-transferrable warranty and are backed by a 10-year support program - nice! Aimed squarely at enduro and eMTB customers, the 2,125g Selva V sits on the scale right in between the Fox 36 (1,920g) and 38 (2,381g) models. Setup and Adjustments The Selva V ships with the gold CTS valve installed, but Formula advised starting by moving up a step to the orange valve for a more aggressive rider. They also proposed leaving the compression and rebound fully open with the ride height set to 20% sag. That’s more sag than I would typically choose. The trails around Squamish are slower, steeper, and generally have a mix of soft organic soil and mineral dirt. For this, I prefer a higher ride height for the gradient, support for larger compressions that want to throw you over the bars, but still need top-end sensitivity to deal with the slippery rocks and roots that can pop up. On faster, firmer, flatter trails, I saw how Formula’s starting point worked well. Through talks with Formula and my own experimentation, I tried numerous setups on both the orange and red CTS valves. Each of those valves has a drastically different feel, keeping all else equal. Formula has an online guide for how to choose the right CTS valve for your style of riding. The language used to describe each valve has a lot of overlap, which makes eight options seem redundant and leads to confusion. Plus, there’s no guidance as to why a rider might close the compression dial or add NeoPos. With more tuning options comes a greater chance of ending up in a suboptimal setting. Even knowing which direction to go in can take a considerable amount of time. Setup #1 Gold CTS 20% sag, decreased to 18, 16% Open compression, closed 2, 4, 6 clicks Open rebound 1 NeoPos Setup #2 Orange CTS 20% sag, decreased to 18, 16% Open compression, closed 2, 4, 6 clicks Open rebound 1 NeoPos Setup #3 Red CTS 20% sag, decreased to 18, 16% Open compression, closed 2, 4 Open rebound Moved from 1 to 2 NeoPos Setup #4 (final) Red CTS 16% sag, increased to 18% (78 PSI) Open compression Open rebound 1 NeoPos Each adjustment made to the fork was done one step at a time, starting with less air pressure and open compression. Performance Either way you chalk it up, the Selva V is a svelte fork (for the travel) and heavy on hydraulics. The orange digressive CTS valve has that feeling of jumping into a puddle. There’s a firm to overcome in the initial stroke. It offers control and comfort when the ride height favors a lower air pressure. I found the orange valve too slow to absorb small bumps effectively, even with the compression and rebound fully open. At the same time, I was searching for more support further into the travel and bottom-out resistance. Increasing the air pressure and later adding a second NeoPos firmed up the fork considerably, but obviously did not solve the sensitivity issues. From there, I switched to the red CTS valve, starting back at 20% sag, 1 NeoPos, and open adjusters. Instantly, the fork was transformed, and sensitivity took shape. A higher ride height and bottom-out support were still needed, so the journey to increase air pressure and add volume reducers started again. I ended up preferring the red CTS valve, 78 PSI (18% sag), 1 NeoPos, and the dials open. The NeoPos volume spacers offer a unique approach. They change the mid-stroke support in a way that no other air fork using plastic spacers can achieve. Through large compressions or steps in the trail, the fork remained higher in the travel and didn’t run into a harsh wall of progression. It wouldn’t be out of the question to cut them in half and install them in smaller increments, though - adding a second is a big change. I’d also be tempted to fit these to other air forks that typically use plastic volume reducers and see how they react. I’ve dragged you through tons of damper and spring talk, but the main thing you’re probably wondering is, are those 35mm stanchions flexible? Unless we put the fork in a jig and measure that, it’s a subjective statement. The front tire, wheel, handlebars, and even the frame can play a role in perceived fork flex. That being said, I didn’t find that to hold me back in any capacity, nor did it noticeably reduce any body fatigue. I never experienced any unusual flex from heavy compressions or through loaded corners. Any comfort claims on Formula’s behalf will be outweighed by air spring and damper harshness found on some air spring or CTS valve combinations. The Formula Selva V, 35mm stanchions, 2,125 g (w/ axle, lever, fender, uncut steer tube): $1,298 USD / €1,250 EUR/ Fox 38 Factory Grip X2, 38mm stanchions, 2,381 grams (29", 170mm travel w/ 190mm steerer + starnut): $1,249 USD / €1,589 EUR. Comparison The Selva V was installed on a Rossignol Heretic enduro bike, which came with a Fox 38 and a Propain Sresh SL eMTB, replacing a Fox 36 . If you are focusing on fore and aft flex, you can notice a difference between larger diameter forks, especially under braking and flat landings. The Formula fork stands out from those two in many ways. First, it has narrower 35mm stanchions than either of the Fox forks, and then comes the damper adjustments. As easy as it may be to twist more knobs in the wrong direction on the Fox, I think the external, on-board adjustments offer a more approachable user experience. Pros + A light chassis option in the long-travel bracket. + CTS valves offer quick and drastic damper changes. + NeoPos volume spacers add more gradual progression to the air spring. Cons - With so many tuning options, it can be overwhelming and time-consuming to find the optimal setup. - Not the plushest ride. - Rebound range is limiting the return speed. - Clunky user experience given the cost. Pinkbike's Take Through all the trials and tribulations with the Selva V, I never did arrive at an ideal setup. A muted, hydraulic sense overrides the fork, giving it a glued-to-the-ground response. Traction and support can be sought out, but if you’re looking for a lively, reactive fork, I’d stick to the usual suspects - they're generally less expensive, too. I believe those damping characteristics match the planted ride of a portly e-bike perfectly, which is somewhat ironic based on the smaller stanchion diameter. — Matt Beer Visit the high-res gallery for more images from this review. Posted In: Reviews and Tech Reviews Forks Formula Formula Selva V Author Info: Matt Beer Squamish, BC, Canada Member since Mar 16, 2001 607 articles Matt has been a Pinkbike reader since the site's early days. He discovered mountain biking in Newfoundland, where a small community of dedicated riders and trail builders set him on a path to earn two Canadian National Downhill Championship titles. Now based in Squamish, BC, Matt works as a tech editor for Pinkbike, specializing in gravity-focused mountain bike reviews, tech, and racing coverage.

title: Review: Northwave Overland Plus Clipless Shoes
author: Matt Beer
article_text: Back in the 90s, Northwave’s red and yellow Integral XC shoes were unmistakable. Today, the colors are a little more subdued in the clipless Overland Plus model, but functionality and performance are still high on the list of objectives list for this all-around trail shoe. The Overland Plus looks to hit the right marks of a solid trail or enduro shoe. There’s a Vibram sole, breathable yet tough outer material, and a stiff sole capped with an EVA footbed. Other highlights include Northwave’s patented SLW3 dial-closure system and a Velcro strap to balance out the tension. Northwave Overland Plus Details • Vibram Fort Augustus rubber sole • EVA midsole • SLW3 lacing dial • Velcro strap • Colours: black and green • Sizes: EU 37-48 • Weight: (per shoe, size 42) • MSRP: $175.99 USD • northwave.com For such a feature packed shoe, you’d expect the Overland Plus to have a hefty price tag, but at $175.99 USD, it lands below most other brands premium models. They’re not as svelte as their modern XC shoes, the Rebel 3 , but they come in at 894g, a respectable number when compared against similar style shoes. Fit Northwave shoes seem to run a bit longer than normal. I typically go for a size 42, but in this case the fit is on the longer side, and unfortunately the Overland Plus comes in full sizes only. Those span from 37 through to 48. The width aligns with most other performance clipless shoes. Although the side panels of the shoe are flexible and the toe box is roomy, they may fit a slender foot best. A high arch also meant wincing through a ride or two before the shoe molded to my foot. Between the Velcro strap at the top and the SLW3 dial around the toes and midfoot, balancing the tension was convenient. A long and cushioned tongue provides ample overlap under the Velcro strap, which meant no pinching at the forefoot. Performance What would make the ideal clipless trail shoe? It would likely have a slim profile that is well vented and be stiff under foot. Offering enough adjustment to suit a variety of foot shapes and pedal cleat positions would be expected too. In the case of the Overland Plus, it nearly checks all of those boxes. Up top, the lightweight material is both breathable and abrasion resistant while the dual closure system holds the foot in place while moving the bike around. It’s flexible enough, but not too squirmy. I really dig this style of closure system; a strap at the top and lace dial in the middle/bottom. The Velcro strap locks your foot in without cutting off circulation, like some other shoes are notorious for. There’s generous padding in the tongue without feeling squishy and vague. Across the lower foot, the SLW3 dial takes in the slack and doesn’t create any pressure points. The SLW3 dial can be loosened, or tightened by a single click, which makes easing the tension a snap during longer rides. Underneath, I have mixed feelings about the sole of the Overland Plus. The stiff shank is preferable for power transfer, however, it lands with a clunky feel while hiking in rocky or rooty terrain. The smooth Vibram rubber doesn’t offer much purchase in soft organic dirt especially in the wet. The cleat box also had a forward bias. For my cleat position preference, I couldn’t move the cleats rearward enough. I felt as if I was standing on my toes while descending, leading to fatigue through the foot and shins (tibialis anterior muscle). Pricing and Comparison Another high-performance clipless shoe that isn’t too far from the style of the Overland Plus is the Fox Union Boa. The latter is slightly more uniform in materials but has an equally stiff sole, comparable venting, and uses a dial closure. Next to the lacing systems and materials, the largest difference is the size of the cleat box. The cleat position can be moved much further back on the Union shoe, even behind the ball of the foot. At $175.99 USD, the Overland Plus is considerably cheaper than the Union Boa but no less durable. If something were to go wrong with either dial system, you’d be more likely to find a replacement for the commonly used Boa at a shop, depending what region of the world you’re in. With that said, online ordering from either brand has never made it easier to make repairs. One small inconvenience of the SLW3 system from Northwave compared to a Boa is that you have to hold the dial’s latch in the open position to unwind the wire and open the shoe. Conversely, the SLW3 can be loosened one position at a time, whereas the Boa used on the Union can only open completely. Both systems can be closed one click at a time. I prefer to pedal uphill, but I’m not adverse to a hike-a-bike. In this case, neither shoe is amazing for walking uphill due to their stiff soles. The Overland Plus locks you foot in for taking on steeper grades whereas the Union Boa can produce some heel lift and chafing. In terms of grip though, the Union’s moderately deep lugs give better traction across all conditions. Durability I’ve been using the Overland Plus on everything from downhill to gravel bikes. Given their on-the-bike benefits, they’ve been treated to more pedal-focused rides and less adventures traipsing through the forest. They’ve been fully submerged on shorter winter rips and haven't been suffocating on longer rides through progressively warmer spring temps. All of the stitching, seams and bonded areas are holding up well. I did manage to snag my toe while climbing and nicked the rubber but that hasn't caused the material to lift any further. So far, the breathable materials on the upper sections haven’t been compromised by any wayward sticks or rocks poking into the trail. All of the stitching and seams are holding strong, plus the Velcro is still grabbing tightly. The SLW3 dial has been trouble free and thanks to its position out of harm’s way, it never saw any impacts either. Pros + Dual closure system allows for fine tuning fit + Dial can be opened one notch at a time for on the move adjustments Cons - Cleat positioning is limited to a forward bias - Lacks grip for hiking Pinkbike's Take Northwave’s Overland Plus is so close to being the ideal mid-weight trail shoe. The dual closure system is one of the best I've tried; a Velcro strap and SLW3 dial can hone in on a personalized fit that locks in your foot without cutting off circulation. Underneath though, it's a different story. Gravity riders will likely wish the cleats could be positioned further back. The stiff sole could benefit from a deeper tread and take the shoe one step further in soft conditions. — Matt Beer Posted In: Reviews and Tech Reviews Shoes Northwave Author Info: Matt Beer Squamish, BC, Canada Member since Mar 16, 2001 607 articles Matt has been a Pinkbike reader since the site's early days. He discovered mountain biking in Newfoundland, where a small community of dedicated riders and trail builders set him on a path to earn two Canadian National Downhill Championship titles. Now based in Squamish, BC, Matt works as a tech editor for Pinkbike, specializing in gravity-focused mountain bike reviews, tech, and racing coverage.

title: Review: Virginia Tech's Top 10 Safest MTB Helmets Ridden & Rated
author: Matt Beer
product_name: Virginia Tech's Top 10 Safest MTB Helmets Ridden & Rated
article_text: Virginia Tech's Top 10 MTB Helmets https://cdn.jwplayer.com/previews/qhWtUAWU Copied Loading ad Live 00:25 00:00 16:56 What makes a helmet “safe”? Is it thicker material, more coverage, or slip planes? From a general physics perspective, more material (considering it’s the correct type) will dissipate the most energy. We’ve seen examples of that theory before - remember the Troy Lee Designs D3 that Cam Zink wore at the 2013 Red Bull Rampage? That was a special use-case, though, plus the average consumer isn't likely to ride around with an enormous bobble-head-like helmet either. That’s not exactly practical, plus extra inertia from a heavier helmet can have other negative effects as well. Helmet manufacturers and other head trauma experts, such as MIPS, have pushed the development of safer equipment in a realistic package. We’ve seen other forms of safety features, especially slip planes, but also designs like 6D’s Omni-Directional System (ODS), and the implementation of variable density materials. Each helmet manufacturer touts their design work, but one entity, Virginia Tech (VT) has been the consumers’ go-to reference to check "how safe a helmet is", or at least, where it stacks up in comparison to other helmets on the market. We’ve rounded up the current top-10 MTB-specific helmets and listed them by their Virginia Tech ranking. Some of those are half-shells, others are ¾ open-face designs, and there’s even one lightweight enduro full-face that made the list. Included is their overall test score, certifications, and technical details. We've also spent time in each one and highlighted the pros and cons. Just want to know which ones we like best? Read our conclusions here . Certifications EN1078: This is a European safety standard for helmets used during cycling, skateboarding, and roller skating. CPSC 1203: The U.S. safety standard for bicycle helmets for persons aged 5 and older, issued by the Consumer Product Safety Commission. AS/NZS 2063: The Australian/New Zealand safety standard for bicycle helmets. NTA 8776: Is a Dutch standard certification given to high-speed e-bike helmets. These are designed to provide increased protection over traditional bike helmets for e-bikes, which reach speeds up to 45 km/h (28 mph). They offer increased coverage and can withstand higher impacts than traditional cycling helmets. How Virginia Tech Conducts Tests The ratings are conducted at their laboratory, independently of the manufacturer. The results are gathered from the data taken from over 24 impact tests conducted from a drop tower. Virginia Tech positions the helmet to receive medium and high energy impacts on 6 locations, measuring the linear acceleration and rotational velocity sustained. The forces are cross-referenced against concussion risk and given a value. Those are added up for an overall score. The lower the score, the fewer concussions the rider would experience, and therefore, the higher the helmet ranks. You can read more specifics about Virginia Tech's testing here . Pinkbike may earn commission on any sales through the affiliate links below. Learn more . #1: POC Cularis BUY AT: Backcountry (USA) Before the Cularis came out, POC was quite far down the Virginia Tech ranking list with the Tectal in 90th, and unfortunately, the beefier-looking Kortal wasn't tested. It was clear that POC had some catching up to do if they were to be more visible on the VTech ranks. Well, they hit a home run with the Cularis as it claimed top spot. It pipped the previous best MTB helmet on there, the Fox Speedframe RS, by a few tenths in scoring. From the outside, and at a glance, there's nothing remarkable about the Cularis - it's quite an average-looking helmet. The coverage is less in terms of EPS foam compared to the Kortal, but that clearly doesn't matter as much when it comes to the impact testing results. Cularis Details • VT score: 7.1 • Fidlock chin strap closure • EPS and polycarbonate shell construction • Mips Air Node • Weight: 360 g • Sizes: S (51–54 cm), M (55–58 cm), L (59–62 cm) • Price: $260 USD • Certifications: EN1078, CPSC 1203, AS/NZS 2063 • poc.com Oddly enough, it doesn't receive the Dutch E-bike safety certification (NTA 8776), which deems helmets appropriate for use over 45km/h. The Cularis uses the newer, slim profile Mips Air Node slip plane built into the sweat liner - no more sharp plastic edges there. It's not the most sweat-absorbing liner, but it does away with any squeaky or sharp plastic inside. It also has a deep fit similar to the Kortal. Glasses storage is found in the forward vents, which feature a fuzzy material to grip those eyewear arms. I wouldn't call it secure, but it does the job if you want to stash them while climbing. The price is getting up there at $260 USD, but should you pick up one of these helmets, you can rest easy knowing it's number one in Virginia Tech's books. You can read more about the Cularis in this full-length review by Seb Stott . The Cularis is a mix of POC's Kortal and Tectal in terms of looks. Even from the inside, the Cularis doesn't show signs of anything unique. Lots of vents up top, yet still the highest rating. The sunglasses' vent storage works well enough to keep them in place uphill. Pros + Secure, deep shell fit. + Mips air node doesn’t have sharp plastic edges. Cons - Doesn't receive NTA-8776 (e-bike) rating. - Air Node liner doesn't have the best sweat absorption. #2: Fox Racing Speedframe RS BUY AT: EVO (USA) The Speedframe RS is the premier half-shell helmet in Fox's lineup. Prior to the POC Cularis, it sat in first place on the VT ranking, and is still a very worthy contender (as are any of the 5-star helmets). It's the only half-shell helmet to use Mips Integra Split, which uses a three-piece, dual-density EPS foam, like Fox's Proframe RS enduro full-face. An inner and outer shell can rotate like one giant Mips slip-plane. The inner shell is even made up of two halves. There's no shortage of features on the Speedframe RS either. The sweat liner is one of the best; the Ionic+ material absorbs sweat well and doesn't irritate my skin, even after long, arduous days. Out back is a BOA Fit System dial to adjust the tension on the fly, while up front is a sunglasses holster. The rubber grippers under the 3-position visor hold them tight enough to descend confidently without them flying out, but locating that stash zone is trickier than it appears. Speedframe RS Details • VT score: 7.23 • Mips Integra Split Dual Density Protection System • BOA Fit System • Fidlock chin strap closure • Ionic+ head liner • Sunglasses storage • Weight: 400g • Sizes: S (51–55 cm), M (55–59 cm), L (59–63 cm) • Price: $319.95 USD • Certifications: NTA-8776 • foxracing.com In terms of fit, it's shallower than Speedframe Pro and 50g heavier. Cost-wise, it's the most expensive at $319 USD, a whole $100 more than the Pro model. The shell is made up of dual-density EPS foams. The inner shell can rotate and is made up of a front and rear half. This bullseye is hard to hit with sunglasses. Once in place, they're locked, though. Pros + Only ½ shell helmet on the market with Mips Split Integra. + Dual EPS construction. Cons - Pricey. - Eyewear storage is tricky to find. #3: Fox Racing Speedframe Pro BUY AT: Jenson (USA) Fox has three new helmet models under the Speedframe heading: a standard model, the Pro, and the RS mentioned above. This one shares the same styling and exterior features of the RS, like the eyewear stowage zone, BOA Fit System, and Fidlock buckle, but the main difference is the shell construction The Pro opts for a Mips Air Node slip plane, opposed to the advanced-looking Integra Split. You still receive the benefit of dual-density EPS foams inside the shell for absorbing variable forces. It's not an inexpensive helmet, however, in my eyes, the Speedframe Pro is a decent value at the higher-end market. Speedframe Pro Details • VT score: 8.1 • Mips Air Node Protection System • BOA Fit System • Fidlock chin strap closure • Dual-density EPS • Sunglasses storage • Weight: 350g • Sizes: S (51–55 cm), M (55–59 cm), L (59–63 cm) • Price: $219.95 USD • Certifications: NTA-8776 • foxracing.com It's worth noting that the fit is slightly deeper than the Speedframe RS. It's lighter and cheaper, too, yet still receives a score of 8.1 on the Virginia Tech ranking scale. Like the Speedframe RS, the visor has three positions. And a BOA Fit System to dial in the tension. The Speedframe Pro has dual-density EPS, like the RS, except it uses the simpler Mips Air Node. The eyewear storage is identical to the RS model. You'll likely need two free hands to store your glasses. Pros + Dual-denisty EPS. + Good value in the high-end price range. Cons - Eyewear storage is tricky to find. - Air Node liner doesn't have the best sweat absorption. #4: Bluegrass Jetro BUY AT: All4Cycling Here's a helmet you don't see too frequently, but then it goes and lands in #4 on the VT rankings. The Jetro is one of three 3/4 helmets on the list and is a solid lid, if you're into that look. It uses Mips Evolve Core for the slip plane, a Fidlock buckle chin strap closure and comes in three sizes. The weight is reasonable at 595g, and the venting is respectable, given the coverage. Bluegrass's own "Safe-T Heta" fit system has a big range with vertical snap adjustments and an easy-to-reach dial. The padding is very comfy and stays in place well when pulling the helmet on and off - something that can be an issue with other helmets on this list. Jetro Details • VT score: 8.41 • Mips Evolve Core • Fidlock chin strap closure • Additional material around critical impact zones • Safe-T Heta retention adjuster • Weight: 595g • Sizes: S (52-56), M (56-5 , L (58-61) • Price: 250 EUR • NTA 8776 certified • met-helmets.com I found a variety of goggles to work with the Jetro, plus there's even sunglasses storage in the vents up top. The Safe-T Heta retention system. Cushy and secure cheek pads along with a Fidlock buckle. For a 3/4 helmet, the venting is decent. Pros + Great airflow for a ¾ helmet. + Secure cheek pads. Cons - Shallower fit than the Fox Dropframe Pro (2023). - Sunglasses fit in vents but aren't totally secure. . #5: 6D Alterra BUY AT: Jenson (USA) The only full-face helmet making the top 10 MTB helmets in the VT ranks is 6D's Alterra. 6D might be best known for their larger volume moto, BMX, and downhill helmets, but they've managed to pack their advanced safety technology into a lighter-weight package. Omni-Directional System is similar in some regard to Mips Integra Split, but absorbs impacts in a 3-dimensional sense, not just rotationally. That's proven to score well in VT's testing, too. On top of that, the chinbar is reinforced with carbon fiber and earns certification for downhill racing. For a lightweight enduro full-face helmet, it also grants a ton of airflow, even with goggles installed. The fit is a bit roomy in some of the rearward areas, but that could be tuned with extra padding. A Fidlock buckle is handy for pulling the helmet off when taking a break at the top, and then back on before dropping back in. Alterra Details • VT score: 8.5 • Fidlock chin strap closure • Omni-Directional Suspension (ODS) • EPP and EPS construction • Weight: 719 g (actual, size M) • Sizes: S (53-56), M (56-59), L (59-62) • Price: $395 USD • ASTM F1952 DH and NTA 8776 certified • 6dhelmets.com Back in the spring, we reviewed the Alterra in depth and were thoroughly impressed with the ventilation and safety tech built into the helmet, given its weight. The recess and padding on the lower edges aim to save clavicles. From the back, the helmet has a bit of a sloping, wide Porsche coupe appearance to it. The padding on the chin strap moves out of the way and the buckle can rest against your jawbone. There's no debating the convenience of a Fidlock helmet buckle. Pros + Impressive venting for a lightweight fullface + ODS system is silent Cons - Chin strap padding rotates to side - Additional padding may be needed to refine fit #6: Specialized Tactic 4 BUY AT: Specialized (USA) What's a helmet that only costs $59.95 USD doing in the Top 10 list? How did Specialized do it? And why would you spend more than that, given its score of 8.55 on the VT scale? Those are all very good questions. Making up the Tactic 4 are a polycarbonate shell, an EPS foam construction, and Mips Evolve Solution - pretty par for the course, yet it's #6. The coverage is great and the weight remains well under 400g. Some answers where the costs were cut lie in some of the less critical materials, and the adjustments. Tactic 4 Details • VT score: 8.55 • Mips Evolve Solution • Breakaway visor • Standard buckle chin strap closure • EPS construction • Weight: 380g • Sizes: S (51-56), M (55-59), L (59-63) • Price: $59.95 USD (on sale from $119.99 • NTA 8776 certified • specialized.com The sweat liner isn't the most absorbent, and, in a previous longer-term review, it delaminated quickly. The chin strap uses a standard clasp buckle, which we can live with; however, the TriFix ear straps aren't adjustable. This can cause the helmet to sit further forward on your brow than you may like. The retention system is a smooth, accessible dial, though, and that offers vertical adjustment, which may alleviate that for you. The best execution of eyewear storage. Dial in the fit. The TriFix strap isn't adjustable. The biggest downside of the Tactic 4 is the liner. This one is intact, but in our previous long-term test, this fell apart quickly. Pros + Unbeatable safety rating for the price. + Best execution of eyewear storage. Cons - Liner delaminates at seams quickly. - TriFix strap is not adjustable and may cause the helmet to sit forward. #7: Sweet Protection Trailblazer Mips BUY AT: Competitive Cyclist (USA) Back in the summer of 2022, Sweet Protection sponsored our Quebec Trail Bike Field Test and sent out Trailblazer Mips helmets, so I've spent a considerable amount of time in this lid . After that test, I continued to wear the helmet because it was comfy, secure, and promoted a ton of airflow. The 4-piece variable elasticity shell technology and Mips Evolve System stack up to give the Trailblazer an 8.69 score on the VT scale, which is nothing to scoff at - it even beats out another one of Sweet Protection's heavy-duty, enduro helmets. Trailblazer Mips Details • VT score: 8.69 • Mips Evolve System, 4-piece shell • 360 Occigrip Fit System w/height and width adjustment • Eyewear storage in front vents • Weight: 325g • Sizes: S/M (53-56), M/L (56-59), L/XL (59-61) • Price: $200 USD • CPSC 1203, CE EN 1078 certified • sweetprotection.com I don't have too many gripes with the Trailblazer Mips, but one of them was the malleable visor. It's not the end of the world, but it can easily sit lopsided. For a helmet that costs $200, that shouldn't be an issue. I'd also appreciate a Fidlock buckle at that price point. The eyewear storage is less refined than their Bushwhacker 2Vi helmet, but it's acceptable. Yellow marks the Mips liner. The flexible visor is durable, however, this one doesn't have any notches to keep it lined up. No Fidlock buckle here. Pros + Great airflow + Fit retention system has height and width adjustment Cons - Would expect Fidlock closure given price tag - Visor can easily be distorted and sit crooked #8: Fox Racing Dropframe Pro (2020 version) BUY AT: REI (USA) If the helmet shape doesn't cause you to raise an eyebrow, then perhaps the model year might. This is the older model Dropframe from 2020. Yes, there's a newer version out now, and looks aside, they are quite different, however, that doesn't detract from its safety ranking. This uniquely shaped helmet from Fox is light for a 3/4 open-face helmet at 485g. Despite coming in four shell sizes, I found the medium to have some pressure points in the upper back half of the shell. The features are on the basic side, too. There's a standard buckle, standard Mips slip plane, and no retention system at all - it relies on padding alone. Dropframe Pro (2020 version) Details • VT score: 8.85 • Standard clip buckle closure • Dual-density Varizorb EPS construction • Weight: 485g • Sizes: S (52-54), M (54-56), L (56-5 , XL (58-60) • Price: $179.99 USD • Certifications: CPSC, EN 1078, AS/NZS 2063 • foxracing.com If nothing else, the Dropframe Pro 202 can be scooped up for some killer deals, and that doesn't detract from the fact that it earns its way into the Top 10. Angular lines and a fixed visor. No BOA Fit System here. Regular old Mips for Princess Leia. And a standard buckle. Pros + Light for a ¾ shell helmet. + Dual EPS foam. Cons - No rention system. - Tight for a medium with small pressure points on top, back corners (there are 4 sizes, however). #9: Fox Racing Dropframe Pro (2023 version) BUY AT: EVO (USA) Landing right behind the 2020 Dropframe Pro is the 2023 version. It's large and in charge at 630g, but there's a ton a features packed into this helmet. Inside the Dual-density Varizorb EPS shell construction is an improved Mips Evolve Core slip plane with an Ionic+ antimicrobial liner. There's also a handy goggle strap recess and a GoPro mount under the 3-position visor. Most notable is the addition of the BOA Fit System, which wasn't featured on the 2020 model, so you can literally dial in the security of the helmet. With that said, the number of shell sizes has been slimmed down to just three, however, the helmet does come with two extra pad kits: one thinner, one thicker. I found the fit to be snug, yet very comfy. The venting isn't as great as the Jetro, and it's best saved for e-biking or cooler weather. Dropframe Pro (2023 version) Details • VT score: 9.03 • Fidlock chin strap closure • Mips Evolve Core • Dual-density Varizorb EPS construction • Ionic+ antimicrobial liner • BOA dial • Weight: 630g • Sizes: S (51-55), M (55-59), L (59-63) • Price: $279.95 USD • Certifications: NTA-8776 • foxracing.com Given the lower levels of ventilation and weight, I'd be more inclined to spend a bit more and go for the 6D Alterra - I like my pearly whites. There is less chance of ending up with a Jerry goggle strap position thanks to the indentations. Lots of vents, but still not the best in terms of ventilation. Mips Evolve Core slip plane and Ionic plus liner. The cheek pads can dislodge when you try to pull the helmet on by the straps. Pros + Snug/comfy fit of a full-face. + Easy to reach BOA Fit System dial. Cons - Cheek pads can dislodge when pulling on the helmet too easily. - Warmer than the previous model. #10: Sweet Protection Bushwhacker 2Vi Mips BUY AT: Competitive Cyclist (USA) Rounding out the Top 10 MTB Helmets in the Virginia Tech Rankings is the big sibling in the half-shell lineup from Sweet Protection. The Bushwhacker 2Vi Mips is a long name with a lot of details packed in. What is 2Vi exactly? This is the name of the technology Sweet Protection gives to the multi-density EPS construction spread throughout the helmet. It's not just randomly placed, but positioned purposefully in common impact zones. The shell is large in volume on the outside and which leads to a deep-fitting helmet. The M/L weighs 430g, has adequate venting, and enough adjustment to suit most head shapes. Bushwhacker 2Vi Mips Details • VT score: 9.05 • Mips Air • 2Vi multi-density EPS construction • 360 fit system • Fidlock closure • Eyewear storage in front vents • Weight: 430g • Sizes: S/M (53-56), M/L (56-59), L/XL (59-61) • Price: $250 USD • Certifications: UKCA, NTA-8776, EN1078:2012, A1:2012, CPSC 1203\ • sweetprotection.com Under the visor are rubber pads in the forward vents to hold glasses tightly. Unfortunately, I found that the visor can shift positions and rattle on rocky descents or head-slapping drops. A precise fit, but the sunglasses storage is solid. The Mips Air liner is as comfy as... well, air. Vertical and lateral occipital adjustments with the 360 Fit system. The visor can jiggle and make noise when charging down the trail. Pros + Snug, wrap around fit + Dual EPS foams in key areas Cons - Visor can rattle in mid position - Excessively long dead-end chin strap loop (I cut mine to avoid choking) Overview Top Choice: Fox Speedframe RS Before the POC Cularis, the Fox Speedframe sat in the top spot of the Virginia Tech ranks for MTB helmets. The Speedframe holds a number of features that make its shell construction more complex with Mips Integra Split, and that seems to have paid off. Fox reworked the shape of the helmet inside, too. In the past, the forehead area didn’t work for me, but now it’s very comfortable. The venting is top-notch, the liner wicks sweat well, and the forward vents hold sunglasses securely enough to ride with. Best Value: Specialized Tactic 4 How could you ignore this helmet? It lands in the top 10 MTB helmets and is currently on sale for $59.95 USD. My favourite part, aside from the excellent safety-to-price ratio of this helmet, is how easy it is to locate the glasses holster - you can place them with just one hand. That’s something I haven’t been able to do with any other helmet (besides the Specialized Ambush). As for that liner, I bet you could retrofit the higher-end Ionic+ fabric from the Ambush helmet into the Tactic with some arts and crafts. Honourable Mention: 6D Alterra Shapes aside, the Alterra is an incredible helmet. Its unique impact protection design, based on Virginia Tech’s testing, proves its worth. For a full-face, it also breathes and vents exceptionally well. Add in the relatively low weight and reasonable price, and you can see why it deserves an honorable mention. Posted In: Reviews and Tech Reviews Ridden And Rated Helmets 6d Bluegrass Fox Racing POC Specialized Sweet Protection Author Info: Matt Beer Squamish, BC, Canada Member since Mar 16, 2001 607 articles Matt has been a Pinkbike reader since the site's early days.
